coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in synthesis, characterization, and environmental applications of activated carbons and other carbon derivatives

Issahaku, Ibrahim
Tetteh, Antonia Y.
coredata.dc:description: The historicity and versatility of activated carbons (ACs) and other carbon derivatives (OCDs) date back to antiquity. This article reviews the recent advances in synthesis, characterization, and environmental applications of these demand-driven adsorbents from biomass and non-biomass sources. It first identifies relevant literature sources and knowledge gaps and then segmentalizes and scrutinizes the theme to elucidate contemporary carbon-based adsorbents. Conventional and advanced syntheses are highlighted. Current trends in adsorbents' characterization and remediation are also presented. The conventional AC synthesis includes one-step or two-step chemical or physical activation or a combinatorial synthesis of the two. Issues in the combinatorics are examined. Advanced techniques such as hydrothermal carbonization/activation and microwave-assisted irradiation are described, which may also involve one-step or two-step procedures. OCDs, including carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, and carbon dots, mainly employ advanced syntheses, for example, nanotechnology. Currently, ACs and OCDs are mainly characterized using advanced techniques like scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), nitrogen (N2) adsorption, and X-ray diffraction (XRD), which elucidate their structures, properties, and potential efficacies. Their applications for removing water, soil, and air pollutants are evaluated. The highest percent removal efficiency was activation type-specific, reflecting the precursor's nature, synthesis method, product properties, and quality. For instance, the chemical activation of neem leaves by H3PO4 activation and Fe nanoparticles from tea extracts were 100% successful for Pb2+and Cr6+, respectively, whereas physical activation of rice husk produced 91.8% success for Cr3+. However, their differential adsorptivities for other metals were moderate, with H3PO4 activation the lowest. Due to the high cost, tedious processes in producing and restoring ACs, and their non-selectiveness, researchers continually search for suitable alternatives, which this review considers. Also, applying artificial intelligence (AI) techniques in advancing novel ACs and OCDs for environmental remediation is discussed. Finally, future research dimensions are proposed.

head.ce:title: Recent advances in synthesis, characterization, and environmental applications of activated carbons and other carbon derivatives

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The historicity and versatility of activated carbons (ACs) and other carbon derivatives (OCDs) date back to antiquity. This article reviews the recent advances in synthesis, characterization, and environmental applications of these demand-driven adsorbents from biomass and non-biomass sources. It first identifies relevant literature sources and knowledge gaps and then segmentalizes and scrutinizes the theme to elucidate contemporary carbon-based adsorbents. Conventional and advanced syntheses are highlighted. Current trends in adsorbents' characterization and remediation are also presented. The conventional AC synthesis includes one-step or two-step chemical or physical activation or a combinatorial synthesis of the two. Issues in the combinatorics are examined. Advanced techniques such as hydrothermal carbonization/activation and microwave-assisted irradiation are described, which may also involve one-step or two-step procedures. OCDs, including carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, and carbon dots, mainly employ advanced syntheses, for example, nanotechnology. Currently, ACs and OCDs are mainly characterized using advanced techniques like scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), nitrogen (N2) adsorption, and X-ray diffraction (XRD), which elucidate their structures, properties, and potential efficacies. Their applications for removing water, soil, and air pollutants are evaluated. The highest percent removal efficiency was activation type-specific, reflecting the precursor's nature, synthesis method, product properties, and quality. For instance, the chemical activation of neem leaves by H3PO4 activation and Fe nanoparticles from tea extracts were 100% successful for Pb2+and Cr6+, respectively, whereas physical activation of rice husk produced 91.8% success for Cr3+. However, their differential adsorptivities for other metals were moderate, with H3PO4 activation the lowest. Due to the high cost, tedious processes in producing and restoring ACs, and their non-selectiveness, researchers continually search for suitable alternatives, which this review considers. Also, applying artificial intelligence (AI) techniques in advancing novel ACs and OCDs for environmental remediation is discussed. Finally, future research dimensions are proposed.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Activated carbons (ACs) and other carbon derivatives (OCDs) are materials derived from carbon-based precursors. They have various forms, structures, and properties, making them suitable for different fields. OCDs include carbon fibers, nanotubes, graphene, biochar, and carbon black [1,2]. AC is a porous, solid, carbonaceous adsorbent synthesized by carbonization and activation of materials made of carbon at high temperatures, around 800 °C, under inert conditions [3]. AC is the most common among carbon-based materials due to its elevated porosity, significant detailed surface areas and chemistry, superior mechanical strength, suitable pore dimensional circulations, and easy regeneration [2,4]. Several parameters determine ACs' surface chemistry and pore structure, including the type of precursors, activation temperature, period, and agent [5]. In addition, other treatments, for instance, oxidation, sulfidation, steaming, metal loading, acidification, and metal oxide integration, significantly influence the advancement of adsorption capacity besides improving carbon selectivity to particular compounds [6].

ACs and OCDs are produced using several carbonaceous materials. The availability, cost, and environment-benign nature of the precursor mainly influence the choice of the precursor, including biomass such as hazelnut shell [7,8], rice straw [9], hybrid willow [10], cotton stalk [11], waste tea [12], oak nutshell [13], pine cone, peanut shell, bamboo, camellia japonica, coconut shell, and jujun grass [8]. Other sources comprise non-biomass like coal [14], wastewater sludge [15–17], waste tires [18], plastic waste [19], polyvinyl chloride [20], asphaltenes [21,22], and petroleum coke [23,24].

The recent synthesis of ACs embraces several methods classified as conventional and advanced techniques. The conventional methods used for activation are usually physical (thermal), chemical, or a combinatorial synthesis of the two. At the same time, the advanced techniques include hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted irradiation, and one-pot synthesis. Manufacturing AC commonly involves one or two stages of carbonization and activation procedures. During carbonization, non-carbon constituents, such as hydrogen and oxygen, are eliminated from the starting material through pyrolytic decomposition, resulting in a skeletal carbon with masked pore assembly [5–7,25,26]. Numerous reviews on AC production in the past few years have been based on conventional and advanced techniques. For example, Ukanwa et al. [27] reviewed the aspect of chemicals to produce AC from agricultural waste biomass, León et al. [28] studied the design, cost estimation, and sensitivity analysis for a production process of ACs from waste nutshells by physical activation, and Jain et al. [29] worked on hydrothermal conversion of biomass waste to ACs with high porosity. On the other hand, Tan et al. [30] evaluated biochar as potential sustainable precursor for AC production with multiple applications in environmental protection and energy storage, and Naji & Tye [31] reviewed on the synthesis of ACs for biodiesel production focusing on precursor, preparation, and modification. However, they failed in their approaches to examine the methodological diversity in the synthesis and activation mechanisms.

The preparation and use of ACs and OCDs in environmental remediation have been the subject of numerous reviews in recent years. For example, Lee et al. [32] reviewed the aspect of recent progress in the synthesis of porous carbon materials, Rodriguez-Reinoso & Silvestre-Albero [33] provided a brief examination of the functions of surface chemistry and porosity in adsorption techniques in addition to a broad summary of the structure, synthesis, characterization, and uses of AC. They also briefly discussed recent developments in synthesizing novel ACs and the growing intriguing confinement effect in the nanopore area for particular applications. Jaiswal et al. [34] emphasized the creation, description, and brief application of magnetic nanoparticle AC. Their review indicated significant advancements in manipulating magnetic nanoparticle size and form, which can be achieved by developing techniques such as chemical co-precipitation, high-temperature treatment, encapsulation, thermal degradation, and hydrothermal synthesis. Also, Gao et al. [35] studied the insights gained from several chemical activating agents regarding AC, and Puziy et al. [36] worked on recent advances in carbon materials from technical lignin. In contrast, Alam et al. [37] looked at the potentiality of rice husk-derived AC from synthesis to application. Similarly, Wong et al. [38] outlined new research and developments on using ACs made from biowaste to remediate wastewater. At the same time, Saleh & Gupta [39] reviewed the properties, processes, and adsorption behavior of tire-derived carbons. However, their reviews did not highlight the methodological variations in the synthesis of ACs and OCDs nor examine the issues with chemical and physical activations in the combinatorial synthesis order.

As a result of growing global environmental challenges and firmer guidelines put in place by governments in both developed and emerging nations, the demand for ACs shall endure. AC is applied in the adsorption of environmental pollutants, commonly in air, soil, and water treatment schemes. For instance, AC has removed Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+ [40,41], methylene blue, and malachite green [23,42] from wastewater. Also, it is used for remediating nitrogen (N2), methane (CH4), and carbon dioxide (CO2) [43,44] from the air, besides the removal of phenanthrene, tween 80 [45], and petroleum [46] from contaminated soils. Among numerous adsorbents, ACs these days are primarily used as an adsorbent for the abstraction of a wide diversity of contaminants in aqueous media or from gaseous milieu owing to their remarkably high surface areas, complex internal microporosity assembly besides the company of a widespread array of surface functional groups. Conventional biological treatment methods are competent in eradicating biodegradable organics. These methods cannot eliminate humic constituents, pesticides, lignin, and residual organic composites of color and odor-forming organics. However, the efficiency of AC in eliminating such organic compounds has been demonstrated [47]. The vast properties of ACs afford them wide applications as electrode materials in electrochemical dual-layer capacitors [7], desulfurization of dibenzothiophenes [6], removal of perfluoroalkyl acids from water [48], to isolate benzenes and cyclohexanes [49], CO2 capture [50], and adsorption of furfurals [51].

Even though AC is preferred for several applications, its wide use is constrained due to its relatively high cost and the tedious production and restoration processes, driving researchers to explore different sources of non-conventional and cost-effective adsorbents [1]. For example, Cossutta et al. [52] reviewed a comparative life cycle assessment of graphene and AC in a supercapacitor application. At the same time, Wang et al. [53] worked on a comparative study of methylene blue adsorption by AC, nickel alginate/AC aerogel, and nickel alginate/graphene oxide aerogel, and Tibor & Grande [25] examined the industrial production of AC using circular bioeconomy principles example from a Romanian company.

AC is also suggested to have slothful adsorption kinetics, nonspecific adsorption, and low competence for microorganisms [54], leading to additional search for alternatives. Another breakthrough in the quest to find a suitable material for remediating the environment has been reached by the ceaseless evolution of technology based on artificial intelligence (AI) and science, leading to the advancement of carbon materials to the nano-scale level [55]. The current advances in carbon nanomaterials deliver various allotropes, including graphenes, diamonds, amorphous carbons, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, fullerenes (C60), and carbon dots for several applications such as electronics, technical, medicine, agriculture, and environment [54–56]. The recent demand for their use is based on their tunable chemical, physical, and optical properties, high electrical conductivity, inherent or artistic electrochemical activity, advanced electrocatalytic and storage presentations via excellent surface area and small pore dimension, biocompatibility, and luxury of functionalization [55,57]. For instance, Cheng et al. [58] combined conservative high surface area AC and conductivity additive with carbon nanomaterials (i.e., carbon nanotubes and carbon nanofibers) using a modest slurry course to advance a novel complex nanocomposite electrode for high-performance supercapacitors, Boujibar et al. [59] fashioned AC having high surface area and tailored nanoporosity with precisely controlled porosity over a modest manufacturing friendly course, producing an exceptional super capacitive presentation with high energy density, Lompe et al. [60] examined the effect of iron oxide nanoparticles upon the adsorption of organic matter on magnetic powdered AC, Beni & Jabbari [61] evaluated nanomaterials for environmental applications, Shokry et al. [62] synthesized nano AC as adsorbent for elimination of dye from simulated textile wastewater, while Long et al. [63] worked on uses of carbon dots in environmental pollution control. However, they did not compare the application efficiency in environmental remediation with ACs.

An additional exciting discovery is the integration of AI in the current advancement of novel AC materials and devices for environmental remediation, which gives promise for the future. The AI approaches that are frequently employed in environmental remediation are artificial neural networks (ANNs), recurrent neural networks (RNNs), convoluted neural networks (CNNs), decision trees (DTs), feed-forward back-propagation neural networks (FFBPNNs), and adaptive network-based fuzzy inference systems (ANFISs) [64]. Because of its many uses, AI has grown to be a powerful instrument for addressing problems in the real world and has garnered a lot of attention. For example, AI techniques have been used in water treatment and desalination to address water contamination and scarcity [64]. By cutting costs and optimizing chemical use, AI can also assist in lowering the operational costs of the water treatment process [64]. Also, Mijwel et al. [65] explored the feasibility of using AI to simulate the adsorption ability of functionalized carbon nanotubes for removing methylene blue. The AI modeling showed an impressive performance using an RNN and a FFBPNN. The modeling results suggest that the adsorption capacity of carbon nanotubes can be predicted accurately, which can improve their effectiveness in eliminating methylene blue from wastewater. Different AI algorithms have predicted how well various adsorbents will work to remove various pollutants from water [66]. AI techniques, in particular ANN, have been used to predict the AC synthesis [67], to assess the qualitative impact of preparation parameters on ACs [68], to estimate the yield and surface area of AC from different biomass sources [69], and to optimize the adsorption process of pollutants [70–73].

Similarly, a new method for removing organic contaminants from drinking water is to use novel devices such as membrane filters, i.e., microfiltration or ultrafiltration, together with powdered AC. This fine powder can adsorb pollutants. For instance, to determine the optimal operating conditions for a specific reactor configuration and water quality, Campos et al. [74] devised a set of mathematical models that could forecast the removal of organic compounds by PAC added to membrane filtration systems.

Further reviews recently dealt with the characterization of AC and OCDs. For instance, AC and OCDs have been characterized using advanced techniques such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy [75–79], thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) [75–77], N2 adsorption-desorption [75,77,78], X-ray diffraction (XRD) [75,78], and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) [75,76]. Other techniques used include Boehm titrations, iodine number, methylene blue index [75,76], adsorption of phenol [75], ultimate and proximal analyses, point of zero charge (PZC), and immersion calorimetry [76]. Additional reviews have recently surveyed the environmental modification and application of AC and OCDs. Their uses in the environment have been extended to comprise (i) heavy metal remediation of polluted waters [80,81]; (ii) removal of (in)organic pollutants in wastewater [82,83]; (iii) adsorption of CO2 and CH4 from biogas [84]; (iv) dye and heavy metals adsorption [85]; (v) removal of natural organic matter from water [86]; and (vi) CO2 capture [77]. Nevertheless, their reviews limited adsorption mechanisms and application to particular environmental pollutants, but the present review is more comprehensive in scope.

The review begins by summarizing the historical authenticity and versatility of ACs and OCDs. It also presents recent advances in synthesizing these carbon-based adsorbents, highlighting methodological diversities, activation mechanisms, and characterizations. It then investigates the applications of these adsorbents in environmental remediation. Another noteworthy contribution comprises a detailed review of multiple adsorption mechanisms of various environmental pollutants. Finally, future research dimensions are proposed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The historicity and versatility of ACs and OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is an essential element of all life in this universe- using carbon for adsorption dates back to ancient times. The Egyptians and Sumerians used carbon in the wood chars (charcoal) custom in 3750 BCE [87]. Charcoal was used in several ways, including domestic smokeless fuel, reduced ores in bronze manufacture, and medical applications [88]. The ancient Egyptians used charcoal to absorb odors from decaying sores and the bowel area. The early Greeks used charcoal to lessen the harmful effects of food poisoning [89]. Filters from charcoal and sand for decontaminating drinking water were known in Hindu documents from 450 BCE. Scheele, in 1773, was the first to observe the definite adsorptive assets of charcoal in the treatment of gases. The first methodical justification of the capacity of charcoal to adsorb in a liquid medium was provided by Lowitz in 1786 [90]. Far ahead, in 1794, a sugar processing plant from England successfully used wood charcoal to decolorize syrups of raw sugar. By 1808, all European sugar processing plants used charcoal as a decolorizer [89].

The first example of producing AC by an amalgamation of chemical and thermal methods was by Bussy in 1822. His-work demonstrated that the decolorizing properties of carbons hinged on the precursor, the processing temperature, and the size of the particles of the end product [89]. The initial industrial-scale fabrication of AC began in the twentieth century. Significant developments occurred in Europe, though it was only obtainable in powdered AC. In 1900 and 1901, two noteworthy methods of preparing and making products from AC were patented by the Swedish chemist von Ostreijko. In that order, they covered the elementary concepts of chemical and thermal or physical carbon activation by metal chlorides in addition to CO2 and steam [90]. Under these patents, the first commercial ACs were produced in Europe: the powdered AC Eponit from wood by gasification approach in 1909 and Norit and Purit from peat with steam in 1911 [91]. The primary use of ACs was for decolorizing solutions in the food and chemical industries. The initial manufacture of AC by the designation filtchar was done in the United States of America in 1913 via waste residues of the paper-making process [89].

Further milestones in the development were reached during the First World War as toxic gases led to improvement and large-scale production of granular AC as a tool for protecting against chemical warfare agents. The poisonous gases were adsorbed by gas masks made from these carbons and were subsequently used for solvent recovery, water treatment, and air purification. Significant progress was made after the First World War in Europe for AC production through novel raw substances of carbon, for instance, almond and coconut shells. Between 1935 and 1940, sawdust by zinc chloride (ZnCl2) activation was used to produce pelletized carbons applied in retrieving volatile solvents besides absorbing benzene from town gas. Of late, the procedure for chemical activation through ZnCl2 has been mainly outdated with the application of phosphoric acid (H3PO4) [89]. OCDs, including fullerenes, were discovered by Kroto and Smalley in 1985, carbon nanotubes by Iijima in 1991, and carbon dots by Xu et al. in 2004 [56,79].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time scale, data sources, and article selection criteria
ce:section.ce:para: The review extends from 2016 to the present, which signifies a critical period in global history characterized by momentous economic, social, political, and technological changes, including environmental concerns. The timeframe is also marked by an ambitious global response to combat climate change effects, the necessity for sustainable policies and practices, and a shift toward an extra environmentally mindful international community. It is, therefore, justifiable to review ACs and OCDs, the significant and indispensable commodities of organic origin, as they demonstrate vital responsibilities in boosting the economy worldwide and have multiple applications in academia, industry, and environment, as the world has embraced circular economy.

The pertinent literature was obtained from Google Scholar, PUBMED, Research4Life, ScienceDirect, and Web of Science built on the keywords "activated," "carbon," "carbon derivative," "nanoparticle," "carbon dot," “carbon tubes,” “carbon fibers,” "synthesis," "mechanism," "characterization," "environmental," "adsorption," "remediation," and "application." Relevant articles were carefully chosen depending on compulsory contributions to AC/OCD development and the journal's credibility, stressing their specific applications to water, soil, and air remediation. From a continental viewpoint, available articles on AC and its applications from 2016 up to date are principally reported by South America (40%), followed by North America and Asia (17%), with the most diminutive Australia (7%) (Fig. 1 ) and most of the pertinent ones were used in line with the objectives of the present review.

The global number of available articles on OCDs and their applications from 2016 up to date are predominantly reported by South America (33%), followed by Asia (18%), with the least Australia (8%) (Fig. 2 ) with the pertinent ones used in line with the objectives of the present review.

Knowledge gaps were recognized after a thorough review of the relevant literature, which facilitated designing a framework for the review. Although considerable research has been done on AC and OCD sources, there is limited elucidation of the methodological diversities in the synthesis. Likewise, there is the rare exposition of issues in the order of combinatorial synthesis involving chemical and physical activations. There is also limited characterization and evaluation of the environmental application of ACs and OCDs from several materials. In addition, the use of AI techniques in advancing novel AC materials and devices for environmental remediation is scarcely mentioned. Similarly, previous reviews on the adsorption mechanisms of these adsorbents limit them to particular environmental pollutants, but the current review is broader in scope.

Carbonaceous materials sourced for producing ACs and OCDs are non-biomass and biomass. Solid wastes from non-biomass, including anthracite, tar pitch, bituminous, waste cartons, waste newspapers, plastics, and industrial by-products, are converted into ACs and OCDs for environmental remediation to improve economic value by providing alternatives to costly ACs and OCDs. An appropriate starting material must be low-priced, readily accessible, and endowed with high carbon content in conjunction with a small fraction of mineral impurities. Furthermore, a significant consideration for opting for an organic precursor for ACs and OCDs consists of the material's efficacy, the most negligible environmental impact, and its storage life [7,8].

An extensive diversity of biomass, including sawdust, rubber leaves, rice shells, wheat shells, lentil shells, wheat straw, and palm leaf powders, have been effectively tried as low-cost adsorbents. The key benefits of such materials include low cost, low sludge production, high process efficiency, and the possibility of metal recovery [92]. The nature of the precursor contributes mainly to the characteristics of ACs and OCDs produced. For instance, ACs produced from lignin-rich precursors (e.g., fruit stones, coconut shells, or wood) result in chars having more significant surface areas and advanced fixed carbon contents than those made from cellulose-rich precursors (e.g., grain husks, straw, or grass) [93]. Additional biomass precursors and their rationale are exemplified in Table 1 .

ACs and OCDs can be prepared from non-biomass, including residue, waste, and natural minerals. For example, Ahmad et al. [123] produced biochar using domestic sludge, while Gombojav et al. [124] and Shokry et al. [62] made AC from coal. Further precursors from non-biomass are represented in Table 2 .

The synthesis of ACs comprises several methods classified as conventional and advanced techniques. The conventional approach includes chemical or physical activation or a combinatorial synthesis of the two to advance and unlock the porosity of biomass or non-biomass materials [5,7,8]. On the other hand, advanced techniques employ, for example, hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted irradiation, one-pot, and carbon dot syntheses [55–58,80,122,140–143].

Conventional synthesis is customarily done using an appropriate precursor subject to chemical or physical activation or a combinatorial synthesis of the two via a one-step or two-step carbonization and activation procedure [5,7,8,139,144]. The one-step activation method involves direct or concurrent progress of both carbonization and activation, while the two-step method starts with carbonization and is followed by activation.

Carbonization, identified as pyrolysis, is typically done by heating the precursor under dipping or inert conditions with temperatures more than 300 °C that differ based on the precursor type. The process converts biomass or non-biomass precursors into carbonized materials with enormous carbon and disarrayed graphite-like carbon structures. Carbonization involves simultaneous dehydrogenation, hydrogen transfer, dehydration, condensation, isomerization, and rearrangement. It gets rid of non-carbon essentials of volatile compounds such as hydrogen (H) and oxygen (O) in the form of water (H2O) vapor, carbon monoxide (CO), and CO2 as gases. This inhibits the development of tar and other unwanted products during carbonization, thereby increasing carbon yield. Parameters such as carbon content, level of carbonization, size, and level of disordered carbon edifice depend on the final carbonization temperature and type of precursor. The quality and yield of the AC are controlled by activation time, carbonization temperature, and heating rate [93,145].

The precursor is carbonized under an inert atmosphere during physical activation, and the carbonized product is subjected to thermal activation using an oxidant gas such as N2, CO2, steam, or their combinatorics at high temperatures [146]. However, impregnation of the precursor with a chemical compound, usually a dehydrating agent, is done during chemical activation. Then, the impregnated carbon is heated in an inert atmosphere. The shortcomings of physical activation are a high temperature and extended time for activation. Chemical activation with one-step and two-step procedures resulted in more modified surface chemistry and needed pore structures compared to physical activation [3,147].

Physical activation is the fractional change of the char into gas with steam, air, CO2, or their combinatorics within 800–1100 ºC temperature in an inert, usually N2 atmosphere [148]. The one-step physical activation process involves the simultaneous conduct of carbonization and activation using common activating gases such as steam, CO2, or combinatorics. However, the two-step process starts with the carbonization of the precursor before activation with the oxidizing gases, which can also be steam, CO2, or a combinatorial synthesis of them. The reaction of steam or CO2 with the carbon structures produces CO2, CO, CH4, or H2. During the activation process, CO2 results in narrow micropores while steam widens the initial micropores of the carbon owed respectively to CO and H2 inhibitors. The high degree of activation results in larger meso‑ and macropore volumes of AC with steam than those prepared by CO2. However, activation with CO2 creates ACs with larger micropore volumes and narrower micropore size distributions than those activated by steam [149–151].

The mechanism for physical activation with CO2 involves the following:
(9)

The formation of CO surface developments begins the reaction (9). The CO surface developments are stable and inhibit the reaction sites (10). Many surface developments can break down into CO, liberating the carbon surface for further reaction (11). The advantages of physical activation compared to chemical activation include less costly and more environmentally friendly [150,151].

Chemical activation synthesizes AC from biomass or non-biomass precursors by impregnating chemical agents and heating at high temperatures. During the one-step chemical activation method, carbonization and activation both progress simultaneously. The precursor is mixed with a chemical that is either acidic, for instance, H3PO4, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3), and zinc chloride (ZnCl2), or basic, for example, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3), potassium hydroxide (KOH), and potassium carbonate (K2CO3) in proper ratios. The chemical activating agents can be used individually or in combinatorics: acid-acid, acid-base, base-acid, or base-base to obtain the desired result. The precursor is then dried and carbonized at 450–900 ºC under a controlled atmosphere such as vacuum or inert gas [3,153–155]. The organic materials at impregnation bond with the activating agents through cleaving aromatic ether bonds improved their interaction. The dehydration and condensation interactions of hydrolyzed glycosidic networks in cellulose and hemicellulose quicken the liberation of gaseous products. As a result, ensuing pyrolysis will modify the precursor's surface area, porosity, and surface chemistry [11]. However, carbonization of the organic material to create biochar at 300–700 °C is done first during the two-step activation method. This process liberates gases and pyroligneous acids and generates graded pores in the biochar. The impregnated and dried biochar is then activated at higher temperatures to produce ACs [3]. Thermal disintegration frees gases, volatile constituents, and tar as pores are formed in the biochar. The tar residues have the tendency to restrict portions of the pores formed. Fortunately, the activator can eliminate these during the next step by creating a microporous carbon construction in the pores [3]. Therefore, the precursor type, activation method, temperature, and time determine ACs' porosity, surface networks, and properties. A comparison of the one-step and two-step chemical activation methods of AC production indicates a higher carbon yield, larger surface area, and micropore volume by the one-step than the two-step method [8,139].

The choice of chemical activation agent significantly influences AC's surface expanse and pore dimensions. KOH activation is extensively explored because it allows the generation of vastly microporous structures and functional collections on carbons. These are owed to the insertion of potassium among the matrices, it being a strong base directly related to carbon to catalyze dehydrogenation and carbon oxidation at high temperatures, resulting in the decomposition of carbon, inhibition of tar and pore creation [8,147,149,154] as shown below:
(12)

The final removal of the proficiently implanted potassium in the carbon matrix by acid washing produces a carbon edifice with exceedingly porous and well-advanced definite surface area [8], as exemplified in Fig. 4 .

H3PO4 has increasingly become used lately for activating lignocellulosic materials for the large-scale production of ACs because it offers some environmental advantages, such as ease of recovery, low energy cost, and high carbon yield. The H3PO4 activation mechanism usually involves it acting as an acid catalyst to enhance bond fission interactions and the creation of crosslinks by progressions, for example, cyclization, condensation, and merging with organic materials to produce phosphates and polyphosphate ester networks with biopolymer fragments [157–159]. The mechanism begins with a reaction of the precursor with H3PO4 to generate polyphosphate ester networks. By activation at 500 °C or below, phosphate networks in the crystal assembly are thermally putrefied and dehydrated, activating lignin and hemicellulose amorphous polymers, creating typically micropores. In contrast, activation of cellulose establishes a combination of pore sizes. The formation of micropores starts at 150 °C, indicating that parts are due to a blend of acid-sponsored interactions and phosphate esters with the amorphous biopolymers. The addition of the phosphate esters creates a course of enlargement, which results in an enlarged pore edifice after the elimination of the acid. The concurrent elimination of carbon as CO2, CO, and light hydrocarbon gases also enhances pore assembly. Increasing the activation temperature converts the amorphous to turbostratic edifice by releasing H and O as gases forming mesopores. The decrease in crosslink concentration permits the development and position of polyaromatic groups, creating a further tightly packed and less porous assembly [160]. However, diverse activation methods compared to identify the best chemical agent performance had impregnation with KOH to produce improved outcomes than H3PO4 [161].

Activation with ZnCl2 leads to the manufacture of ACs accompanied by good yield and well-developed porosity. ZnCl2, as a Lewis acid, has a diverse activation mechanism from the other activation reagents. ZnCl2 acts as a robust dehydrating agent that can react with O in OH clusters, creating dehydration and dehydrogenation reactions from the hydroaromatic assembly of the organic precursor. During heating, ZnCl2 becomes fused at its melting point of 290 °C and reacts with H2O produced from the division of the biopolymer, resulting in zinc oxide chloride hydrate (Zn2OCl2 ·2H2O). Primarily, Zn2OCl2 ·2H2O disintegrates because of higher temperatures to give off ZnCl2 gas, significantly developing several novel sites, successive advances in pore structure, and porosity formation in the finished product. Furthermore, ZnCl2 gas limits tar creation in the designed pores [154,162]. The mechanism is as follows:
