ACs produced by ZnCl2 activation have higher surface areas and larger pore volumes than KOH activation [154,162]. The main disadvantage of chemical activation is the pollution of the environment as a result of chemical use and chemical washing [31]. The advantages of chemical activation over physical activation include quicker activation time, lesser activation temperature, and more yield.

AC can also be synthesized by combining chemical and physical activation methods [82,163,164]. However, there is no definite synthesis order by combining chemical and physical activations. For example, recent publications by [103,165] combined the specific physical and chemical activation properties in the order of physical followed by chemical activation, significantly improving AC's adsorption performance. Other late publications using a combined chemical and physical activation synthesis used chemical followed by physical activation [166,167]. Most relevant articles on combined methods are chemico-physical, where chemical activation is followed by physical activation, with very few studies on the physico-chemical combination (i.e., physical activation followed by chemical activation). The combinatorics can be paired as (i) a one-step chemical (acid, base; acid-acid; acid-base; base-acid; or base-base) followed by physical (steam, CO2, steam-CO2, or CO2-steam), (ii) one-step physical followed by chemical, (iii) two-step chemical followed by physical, and (iv) two-step physical followed by chemical. For instance, a recent synthesis produced AC from a combined chemical-physical activation by a two-step treatment method [168]. The first step involved chemical amendment of the oil fly ash employing diverse mixtures of H2SO4 and H3PO4, followed by physical activation with CO2 at high temperatures.

One of the major issues with combinatorics is selecting appropriate physical and chemical activation agents from an infinite number of agents to ensure the desired results. This challenge can be addressed using AI. Besides selection issues, the techniques are laborious, time- and energy-consuming. The economic and environmental costs are also huge, not underrating the hazards and risks associated with the activating agents in the occupational or research environments. These issues apply equally to standalone physical and chemical activation methods but perhaps on a lower scale.

Novel AC synthesized by chemical, physical, and combined chemical-physical activation was examined [164]. The combined chemical-physical activation was done by taking the chemically synthesized AC with KOH through physical activation with CO2 at 850 °C for 20 min. Comparison of the result of the activation method on the properties of AC indicated the highest yield by physical activation with CO2 at 850 °C with yields of 87 wt.% followed by 70 wt.% yields for KOH at 700 °C and the least with 57 wt.% for combined chemical-physical activation. Between the established activation procedures, AC with the most advanced surface area and micro/meso‑porosity was formed by chemical activation with 493.75 m2/g and 0.48 cm3/g, respectively, followed by combined chemical-physical activation with 490.28 m2/g and 0.72 cm3/g and the least of 164.33 m2/g and 0.50 cm3/g by physical activation.

Combined chemical-physical activation of palm shell by conventional one-step chemical impregnation and activation with ZnCl2 and H3PO4, the resulting products additionally activated by conventional physical activation with CO2 were subsequently studied [169]. The results discovered that H3PO4-saturated samples achieved improved activation dimensions than those treated with ZnCl2 or the physically activated ones. Applying a small quantity of ZnCl2 and H3PO4 generates nearly tiny pores that can be advanced evenly by further physical activation, with surface areas of 1118 m2/g and 1653 m2/g, respectively. Likewise, blending these two activation methods produces extra homogenous pore size circulation. With an immense surface area, AC was produced by combining the chemical activation method with H3PO4 and physical activation via N2 using durian peel waste [167]. H3PO4 activation was renowned as a decent acid for refining the AC worth. Physical activation with N2 impacted the development of pores. Hence, combining the physical and chemical activation methods will produce a synergetic effect of the two syntheses, including strong basic and acidic functional assemblies.

The advanced techniques employ novel processes to tune known ACs and OCDs' chemical, physical, and optical properties to the desired structures and dimensions. The techniques include hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted irradiation, solvothermal method, one-pot synthesis [55–58,80,122,140–143], and AI techniques such as ANN [67].

An alternative carbonization is hydrothermal carbonization or wet pyrolysis, which involves heating the carbonaceous precursors, for instance, carbohydrates (e.g., glucose) or biomasses in water by autoclaves at reaction temperatures <300 °C at high pressures to acquire reordered, re-structured, recreated, and carbonized forms of the carbon precursors [140,170]. This varies the water's physical properties, reducing the dielectric constant and making it less polar. The carbon precursors endure dehydration, re-polymerization, and depolymerization to become extra-carbonized products termed hydrochars [140]. Usually, the core products using hydrothermal procedures are solid (hydrochar), liquid (bio-oil), and gas outputs (syngas such as CH4, CO, and CO2) [171]. The resultant chars are lignite-like outcomes branded by an advanced sum of functional oxygen groups existing on the surface with characteristically small specific surface areas [170]. Hydrothermal carbonization is less energy exhaustive than dry pyrolysis, particularly for wet biomass, as the energy-concentrated drying step is evaded. Similarly, throughout hydrothermal carbonization, the physical and chemical reordering of carbon precursors begins at a lesser heating temperature in water (180 °C) compared to pyrolysis (350 °C) [140].

The hydrochars obtained from hydrothermal carbonization can be activated by chemical or physical activation or a combinatorial synthesis of the two in a physical-chemical or chemical-physical activation procedure to enhance the surface area and porosity.

Microwave-assisted irradiation is an advanced physical technique in which the samples absorb electromagnetic energy with a volumetric frequency between 0.3 GHz and 300 GHz and then convert it to energy. Subsequently, the produced heat is uniformly circulated to the constituents, improving the features of the AC. This technology for producing AC from biomass and non-biomass has been rising lately due to some benefits over the conventional heating method. Conventional heating involves heating from the surface to the interior portion of each constituent, resulting in a temperature gradient. The thermal gradient creates distortion and non-homogeneous microstructure in the AC. The thermal gradient can be evaded by slow heating. But that typically results in a long production course, requiring greater energy use and increased cost. However, microwave heating directly interacts with the microwaves and the particles in dielectric heating, resulting in rapid volumetric heating. Other advantages of microwave-assisted irradiation include (i) discriminatory heating, (ii) energy savings, (iii) overall cost efficiency, (iv) a decrease in waste volume, (v) flexible and fast procedures with possibility of remote control, (vi) ease of control, (vii) greater chemical reactivity, (viii) contacting heating (ix) the capacity to treat waste in-situ, (x) rapid start-up and stoppage, (xi) portable equipment and methods, (xii) higher safety and automation, and (xiii) cleaner energy source [172–175].

Microwave heating is likely to convey energy at the speed of light and does not comprise heat conduction or convection [172–175]. Microwave-assisted chemical and physical activation methods can produce ACs with advanced surface area and pore structure [26,174,175]. Microwave-assisted irradiation can consist of chemical or physical activation or a combinatorial synthesis of the two via a one-step or two-step carbonization and activation procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ANN model prediction of AC synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Wang et al. [67] used a tubular reactor to produce AC from different raw materials and studied the effects of activation temperature, time, steam/char and CO2/char ratios. They also developed a novel ANN model with nine input variables to predict AC production. They used 113 data sets to train, test, and validate the ANN model. They found that logsig was the best transfer function for the hidden layer, and 15 was the optimal number of neurons, resulting in mean square error (MSE) and coefficient of determination (R2) values of 0.010967 and 0.967, respectively. The back propagation-ANN model effectively predicted the AC yield and surface area for physical and chemical activation. The model predictions were highly accurate for steam activation and consistent with the experimental CO2 and KOH activation trends. They also analyzed the impact of input variables on outputs. They found that oxygen content, activation temperature, and CO2/char ratio were the most important factors for AC yield and surface area.

To assess the qualitative impact of preparation parameters on ACs made from local anthracite coal, a study by Rashidi et al. [68] provides an ANN with ANFIS models. The iodine numbers were anticipated for this purpose by adjusting key factors under several experimental setups. Initially, four criteria were selected based on prior findings. After the procedure, it was determined that agent type, ratio, activation temperature, and time were the most successful. Three of these previously mentioned parameters, agent ratio, activation temperature, and activation time, were later identified as the most significant with the aid of ANFIS models. Furthermore, reducing the number of experiments required may serve as a springboard for developing more accurate methods of simulating the effective parameters in creating AC. This results in time and cost savings. Liao et al. [69] built multi-layer feedforward ANN models to estimate AC yield and surface area from different biomass sources by pyrolysis and steam activation. They used 168 data samples from literature experiments to train, validate, and test the ANN models. The ANN models had high accuracy and matched well with the experimental data.

OCD synthesis mainly employs advanced technologies to improve the physical and chemical properties of the adsorbents. OCDs are considered emergent materials due to their exceptional chemical and physical properties, varieties of carbon-based assemblies, and the formation of innovative prospects in numerous subfields of chemistry, physics, and engineering [2]. OCDs include carbon nanotubes, graphene, biochar, carbon nanofibers, carbon dots, and carbon aerogels [1,2,79]. Carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, and carbon fiber syntheses exemplify this section.

Carbon nanotubes are nanomaterials with a cylindrical carbon assembly having an extensive range of tunable optical, electrical, and physical properties. They have typical dimensions ranging from 0.4 nm to a few tens of nm with the carbon atoms sp2-hybridized. According to Zaytseva & Neumann [46], carbon nanotube synthesis is widely done by the arc discharge method, which consists of dual extremely pure graphite electrodes met with a distance of 2–3 mm. One of the electrodes comprises metal catalysts including Co, Fe, Mo, or Ni. The reaction compartment's direct current arc discharge is formed over the He atmosphere. This leads to the graphite and metal catalyst evaporating and condensing on the cathode, creating carbon soot with single and multi-walled carbon nanotubes. Producing single and multi-walled carbon nanotubes depends on the type of metallic catalyst chosen [54,56].

Chemical vapor deposition is among the most explored and regularly used methods for synthesizing carbon nanotubes [54]. Hydrocarbon precursors such as acetylene, methane, ethane, alcohols, or ethylene disintegrate at elevated temperatures with a metallic catalyst, e.g., Co, Ni, Fe. The reaction gas is taken over the flow furnace at around 1000 °C, crumbling the hydrocarbon fragments to active carbon species on the catalyst surface. Two diverse developments can then arise: in the tip-growth model, the catalyst–substrate interface is frail. The disintegrated hydrocarbons diffuse down over the metal, and the carbon nanotube grows between the catalyst and substrate. The base-growth model's catalyst–substrate interaction is robust with a base growth course. Once more, the disintegrated hydrocarbons melt on the catalyst surface, and then the sleet must occur from the metal's tip with the sp2 edifice. The procedures end once the catalyzer constituents are enclosed with surplus carbon [54,56].

Carbon nanofibers are non-continuous, one-dimensional carbon nanomaterials typically cylindrical, including stacked cups, cones or plates, and bent graphene leaves established in myriad approaches. Carbon nanofibers generally are clarified as sp2-based hybridization with a 50–200 nm diameter. Carbon nanofibers have special assets like advanced electrical and thermal conductivity, large surface area, and are economical. Carbon nanofibers can be produced using two critical methods: electrospinning and carbonization of polymers or catalytic chemical vapor deposition [176–178]. The most suitable process depends on the anticipated features of the carbon nanofibers. The catalytic chemical vapor deposition permits the mass manufacture of carbon nanofibers by engaging transition metals, for instance, Co, Ni, and Fe, as primary catalysts for the alteration of several hydrocarbon gases (CO, CH4, C2H4, C2H6, and C2H2) [178]. This reaction is usually done at moderately elevated temperatures at 500−1000 °C and reliably creates carbon nanofibers over the catalytic decomposition of hydrocarbon gases [178].

Electrospinning involves using a robust electrostatic field to a capillary linked to a polymer solution reservoir [176]. This is an effective method to form polymeric nanofibers, permitting the molding of the chemical configuration, fiber morphology, functionality, and fibrous construction. Electrospinning formulates nanofibers from tens of nanometers to a few micrometers in diverse procedures, such as yarns, nonwoven mats (webs), etc. It is a comparatively modest and low-cost approach to manufacturing continuous nanofibers from polymer solutions or melts. The key electrospinning production parameters are the spinning voltage, the concentration of the polymer solution, the solution feeding rate, and the working distance, which induce the polymeric fibers' morphology. Carbon nanofibers are synthesized by spinning polymeric precursor fibers, tailed by thermal action. The size of the fiber reduces with rising carbonization temperature. Solvents usually used are acetone, CS2, CHCl3, cyclohexane, tetrahydrofuran, water, dimethylformamide, and toluene [176].

Carbon dots are novel carbon nanomaterials with dimensions below 10 nm, possessing characteristics such as fluorescence, surface functionalization, low toxicity, and biocompatibility. Carbon dots can be synthesized using two main methods: top-down and bottom-up approaches [57], using known ACs or OCDs. The top-down approaches include the cleavage and exfoliation of carbon precursors, for instance, carbon fibers, graphite, coal, and carbon nanotubes. The processes applied in the top-down method include hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, oxidative cleavage, electrochemical synthesis, ultrasonic-assisted, and microwave technologies [55,57]. The bottom-up procedures comprise carbonizing small organic molecules such as glucose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, and fructose. These can be combined with further molecules yielding heteroatom doping atoms such as sulfur, boron, and nitrogen. The carbon dots' surface can be altered during or after synthesis through functionalization, heteroatom doping, or surface passivation [57]. The competence of the top-down course includes the likelihood of increasing the manufacture of carbon dots and distinct carbon dot networks. Conversely, the bottom-up approaches are more challenging to control since the resulting byproducts necessitate extra refinement. Nevertheless, bottom-up methods have competence with a modest surface amendment in one pot and the production of a diversity of structures and functionality of carbon dots that are different from those produced with top-down approaches, which look like the structure of their precursor [57].

ACs and OCDs are characterized using several methods, including N2 adsorption-desorption, iodine number, methylene blue value, Boehm titrations, determination of the pH of the zero-charge point, SEM, XRD, FTIR spectroscopy, TGA, and Raman spectra for product elucidation. This review, which focuses on SEM, N2 adsorption-desorption, XRD, FTIR spectroscopy, and TGA, will lead to an understanding of their fine structures and reflect other properties routinely investigated, such as iodine number, methylene blue value, density, surface area, and pore size distribution, useful for several applications.

Using SEM for their surface morphology, AC and OCD products can be imagined [179]. Lei et al. [179] identified a coarse morphology and a characteristic porous edifice for AC prepared by H3PO4 activation using Zanthoxylum bungeanum branches as a precursor. This spectacle was credited to H3PO4's exceptional thermal stability, which permeates into the interior of the precursor, accelerating the carbonization of polysaccharides from plant filaments to produce an H3PO4 polymer network in the activation course. The porous edifice is formed during the heat treatment of the network when H3PO4 and biopolymer cross-link via a phosphate ester bond, averting the contraction of the cell wall. Nevertheless, a uniform and smooth surface without precise pore assembly was observed for the sample without an activation agent.

Using SEM micrographs, Rehman et al. [139] described the consequence of KOH activation on porous and morphological assembly of AC from terephthalaldehyde and o-phenylenediamine. KOH activation led to a unified porous web at 600 °C with a vastly compact web detected at 800 and 900 °C. The rise in activation temperature is ascribed to the widespread porous web creation to the harmonious outcome of KOH etching and intercalation of potassium and carbon matrix vaporization. The porous webs delivered to cavernous lying nanopores accord competent circulation conduits for gas particles. AC made from sawdust-consequent hydrochar by chemical activation through a combination of potassium oxalate (K2C2O4) and melamine (C3H6N6) was observed using SEM [180]. It was detected that the activated elements, irrespective of the preparation circumstances, upheld the morphology characteristic of the sawdust-consequent hydrochar with a blend of microspherules and filaments on their surface comparable to that attained by K2C2O4. This observation is contrary to results for activated elements prepared with KOH activation, for which there is a total alteration in the morphology of the initial elements (Fig. 5 ). The holding of the unique morphology ensuing K2C2O4 activation establishes a possible gain over the usage of KOH consistent with potassium bicarbonate (KHCO3), and KHCO3 plus melamine, yielding particularly a valuable morphology being, for instance, nanosheet-like or spherical. The composition and surface morphology of AC from maize tassel, using H3PO4 activation, was examined to have a sophisticated pore scheme with small- and mid-sized pores [97].

The morphology of chemically activated biochar from sorghum distillers' grain, by KOH and NaOH, was categorized by SEM [181]. The results reveal that the two biochars have an apparent porous edifice, corroborating both activators' pore-creating outcomes. SEM micrographs of AC from pineapple crown wastes presented porous, extra-dense constituents and a smaller pore span [182]. Also, asymmetrical spherical elements were distinguished, with the surface looking exceedingly porous and heterogeneous, with diverse dimensions and irregular frameworks, triggered by the evaporation of NaOH in carbonization, leading to a prolonged state with a pore assembly and reducing the pore diameter. SEM results for the surface morphological edifice of pineapple crown AC displayed carbons having pores among particles that appear lengthened and asymmetrical [183]. The units fashioned were dependent on the activation time; increasing activation time delivers ample opportunity for the development of carbon and CO2 reactions to facilitate the termination of the carbon chain. The carbon chain termination causes cracks and faults in more extensive constituents.

The SEM images of AC from waste tires by steam activation show heterogeneous and porous surfaces [184]. Also, SEM examination of the surface and appearance of AC from waste tires after steam activation revealed a vastly irregular rough surface with exceedingly dispersed asymmetrical cavities on the surface, attributable to the destruction of the volatile components and steam activation [185]. The structure of the surface of AC formed from waste tires by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) activation exhibited a porous morphology, indicating that the H2O2 activation critically advanced the properties of the precursor regarding the vastly improved porous structure. The porosity of the AC was obvious on the external surface occupied by voids, attesting to a high surface area with relatively even pore circulation and the shape and size presenting a mesoporous and microporous nature [186]. SEM micrographs for carbon nanotubes from coconut shells indicate they closely follow the shape of the surface they are on because of the weak force between them and the surface [187]. This is called the van der Waals attraction. The materials had different sizes and angles. The sizes are 222 nm (θ = 30°), 179 nm (θ = 83°), 384 nm (θ = 62°), 670 nm (θ = 5°) and 187 nm (θ = 72°).

Bundles of graphene and multi-walled carbon nanotubes were found on the surface heated to 700 °C. The SEM images of single-walled carbon nanotubes showed how the fibers are arranged in the nanocomposite sample [188]. In the sample without single-walled carbon nanotubes, the fibers were separated from the surface where the light bends. The fibers stick out from the surface evenly and firmly in the sample with single-walled carbon nanotubes. This makes the nanocomposite stronger and more resistant to breaking. The single-walled carbon nanotubes also act as bridges across the cracks. This is called the crack pulsation mechanism. The amount of single-walled carbon nanotubes affects the mechanical properties of the nanocomposite. More single-walled carbon nanotubes mean less energy absorbed by the failure. Functionalizing carbon nanotubes is also important because it creates a weak bond that contrubtes to toughness. It also fills the nanocomposite's pores and affects the fiber's strength. SEM images were used to examine the shape and size of composite nanofibers from polyethylene oxide/CuO/multi-walled carbon nanotubes [189]. SEM pictures of different materials showed pure polyethylene oxide having a flaky and dense structure Fig. 6 . This means it is crystalline, as XRD analysis also confirmed. The images show CuO nanoparticles, which are small and round to be evenly spread. Also, multi-walled carbon nanotubes were twisted and tangled because of functionalization. Polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes with 1% and 3% of fillers indicated smooth fibers, which means the fillers are well mixed with the polymer. However, polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes (3%) have some lumps, which may be due to too much CuO and multi-walled carbon nanotubes.

The pore construction of AC adsorbents can be examined using a surface area analyzer via N2 adsorption-desorption at 77 K [179]. Lei et al. [179] established a blend of type I and IV isotherms for AC from Zanthoxylum bungeanum branches, with a seeming hysteresis loop of type H4, signifying the concurrent occurrence of micro- and mesoporous construction. The micropore edifice is characteristic of type I isotherm, while the N2 capillary condensation in the mesoporous structure is linked to the H4 hysteresis loop. The H4 hysteresis loop is usually detected in micro-mesoporous carbon substances having slit-like pore assembly. The isothermal gradient echoes the similitude of mesopore spreading, where superior homogenous pore assembly corresponds to a higher slope. The pore size supplies of the produced adsorbents displayed a bimodal order focused at 0.8 and 3.0 nm, indicating the adsorbents parade micro-mesopore features. The adsorbents all comprised high specific surface areas, ≥ 585.6 m2/g, significant pore volumes, ≥ 1.26 cm3/g, and extensive average pore size, ≥ 6.54 nm.

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of activated biochar from sorghum distillers' grain were presented using NaOH and KOH activation [181]. Isotherms fitted to type IV were identified, demonstrating that the biochar possessed mesoporous construction. At a relative pressure of more than 0.95, the surge in the adsorption–desorption isotherms of NaOH-activated biochar become extra apparent relative to that of KOH-activated biochar, signifying further macropores in the NaOH-activated biochar. An advance-magnification analysis shows that NaOH-activated biochar contains a thin, cracked pore wall assembly. In contrast, KOH-activated biochar parades a continuous porous construction with moderately thick pore walls. Because both biochar categories were attained under similar temperatures, the palpable variance in porous assemblies is primarily ascribed to the diverse activation effects of NaOH and KOH. The pore textural assets of pineapple crown leaf, AC, using N2 physisorption, determined that the isotherm presented hysteresis loops and seemed to blend Type I and IV isotherms by Brunauer's cataloging [190]. It was resolved that the substance comprised micropores and mesopores. The main persistent peak was around 1.79 nm, and most pores were smaller than 3.24 nm. The proportion of micropore volume to total volume amplified from 10.33 to 46.02% after the activation course, demonstrating the activation step created a microporous construction. The textural assets of AC fibers from pineapple plant leaves by merging KOH activation and microwave heating were examined using N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K [182]. The isotherm for the fibers was branded as type IV, typical of materials with keen mesoporous construction. The desorption progression displayed an H3-hysteresis distinctive of capillary condensation development detected in mesoporous constituents. The pore size delivery of the activated fibers showed that most pores were dispersed in the dimensional range of 25–50 Å (2.50–5.00 nm), approving the mesoporosity features of the constituents. Further characteristics of AC from biomass for specific surface area and total pore volume are exemplified in Table 3 .

The comparative results on the effect of biomass precursor and activation method indicate that ACs by conventional physical activation exhibited specific surface area and total pore volume in the ranges 236.9 – 1141 m2g–1 and 0.23 – 1.44 cm3g–1, respectively, while those for chemical activation were 304.73 – 2210.7 m2g–1 and 0.13 – 3.18 cm3g–1. Also, ACs by microwave-assisted physical and chemical activations gave values for specific surface area and total pore volume, respectively, as 1350 – 2288 m2g–1, 1356.25 – 1552 m2g–1, and 1.07 – 1.299 cm3g–1, 0.696 – 1.75 cm3g–1. Hydrothermal carbonization presented 139.5 – 1251.5 m2g–1 for a specific surface area. This implies that for biomass precursors, microwave-assisted physical activation showed the highest specific surface area, while conventional chemical activation gave the highest total pore volume.

The specific surface area analysis for ACs produced from waste tires by steam activation demonstrated that the method can generate pores in micro and meso dimensions on the product surface. The size and total pore volume show that many are micro-sized. The category of hysteresis ring identified the geometric shape of the pores formed by steam [185]. Additional characteristics of AC from non-biomass precursors for specific surface area and total pore volume are represented in Table 4 .

The comparative results on the effect of non-biomass precursor and activation method show that ACs by conventional physical activation exhibited specific surface area and total pore volume in the ranges 97 – 1591.72 m2g–1 and 0.0801 – 1.223 cm3g–1, respectively, while those for chemical activation are 10.3 – 3550.7 m2g–1 and 0.415 – 2.62 cm3g–1. Also, hydrothermal carbonization resulted in 95.76 – 279.59 m2g–1 and 0.11 cm3g–1, respectively, for specific surface area and total pore volume. Those for the nanocarbons were 33.5 – 1782 m2g–1 and 0.08 – 2.55 cm3g–1. These indicate that conventional chemical activation produced the highest values for specific surface area and total pore volume.

A comparison of the results for biomass and non-biomass precursors demonstrates that conventional chemical activation of non-biomass precursors presented the highest specific surface area. In contrast, conventional chemical activation of biomass precursors resulted in the highest total pore volume.

The crystalline nature of AC is analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer [179]. Lei et al. [179] observed AC from Zanthoxylum bungeanum branches, a broad diffraction band at 2θ = 22.5° attributed to the characteristic crystalline plane of disordered graphite-form carbon edifice. The band at 2θ = 22.5° for other samples swung perceptibly to an advanced angle of 24°, disclosing the interlayer d-spacing progressive drops due to a rise in graphite crystallinity. The XRD spectrum for Zea mays tassels AC illustrated a continuous function with moderately fixed peaks at 2θ = 22.6 and 26.2° distinctive of an amorphous substance [98].

The XRD pattern of the AC of pineapple leaves revealed an amorphous nature [179]. The diffraction patterns display two broad and low-strength peaks at 2θ site of 25.4° and 43.4°, likened to the graphitized turbostratic (002) and (100) spacing of carbon assembly. The feeble peaks indicate a low graphitization of the activated sample. The edifice can be of 2D order shaped by analogous alignment of carbon layer planes. The XRD designs of AC fibers from pineapple plant leaves displayed two discrete peaks [182]. The broad peak at 26° typical of disordered aromatic carbon edifice was allocated to reflection plane C (002), while the peak at 43.4° of graphitic or ordered carbon was to reflection plane C (100/101).

The XRD pattern of AC from waste tires by steam activation displays broad and strong peaks at 2θ =24°−25° and 42°−43°, matching (002) and (100) reflections of amorphous carbon [184]. XRD was used to examine CuO nanoparticles, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, and polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes [189]. The XRD pattern of CuO nanoparticles showed two peaks at 35.57 and 38.85°, which means they are very well ordered. The XRD pattern of multi-walled carbon nanotubes peaks at 25.70°, which means they were also very well composed. The XRD patterns of polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes with 1% and 3% fillers show the peaks of polyethylene oxide and multi-walled carbon nanotubes. For polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes with 1%, the peaks of multi-walled carbon nanotubes and CuO are weaker because there is less filler in the polyethylene oxide. For polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes with 3%, the peaks of multi-walled carbon nanotubes and CuO are stronger because there is more filler in the polyethylene oxide. This means the composites keep their order after adding multi-walled carbon nanotubes and CuO nanoparticles.

The physical properties, nature, and chemical reactivity of the surface functional groups on ACs and OCDs expound their electrochemical assets. Abdel et al. [231] considered the FTIR spectra within 500–4000 cm−1 for different adsorbents prepared from African beech sawdust. They found peaks at 3500–3200 cm−1 typical for -OH stretching vibration in hydroxyl groups. These peaks were faint, signifying that during carbonization and chemical activation of sawdust, categories of bond fission happened, causing elimination and dehydration reactions, which liberate volatile materials, for instance, acetic acid, methanol, water, and added chemical constituents. Other bands at 1650–1450 cm−1 for all the produced carbons were allotted to C = C stretching in aromatics, while bands at 1000–1100 cm−1 were allotted to C–O and C–O–C stretching. The bands at 600–900 cm−1 were credited to hydrogen bending in several sites in the aromatic rings.

Lei et al. [179] investigated the FTIR bands for ACs from Zanthoxylum bungeanum branches, produced with different heating rates using H3PO4 activation. Peaks located at 1200 and 3400 cm−1 were ascribed to several hydroxyl groups with OH stretching vibration, while bands at 1650 cm−1 were attributed to many carbonyl groups with robust C = O stretching vibration bands. It was concluded that the nature of available collections does not vary with the rise in heating. Instead, the composition distinctly changes. FTIR spectra for Zea mays tassel AC presented diverse functional groups, for instance, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amide [98]. Bands for stretching vibrations for NH and OH of polymeric compounds were detected at 3410.200 and 3228 cm−1. The bands at 1400.86 and 1610.77 cm−1 matched respectively to NH bending vibrations characteristic of carboxylate fractions, carboxylic sets, and C = O stretching. The bands for CH stretching vibrations of carboxylic acids were noticed at 2924.45 cm−1 and 2928.54 cm−1, while that for S = O was at 1103.61 cm−1. The FTIR spectra of AC fibers from pineapple plant leaves confirmed numerous bands; the wide band positioned at 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the OH stretching of hydroxyl clusters [182]. The band between 1600 and 1580 cm−1 was ascribed to CC vibrations in aromatic rings and C = O stretching vibration of carbonyl and carboxyl sets. The band at 500–600 cm−1 is credited to the aromatic structure. Typically, bands ranging from 1000 to 1300 cm−1 are allocated to oxidized carbons, as CO stretching vibration of alcohols, carboxylic acids, phenols, esters, and ethers.

FTIR spectra of the produced AC show six major absorption bands at 3415–3442, 2917–2923, 2360–2426, 1587–1639, 1382.73, 1269–1271 and 450–750 cm–1. The wide band at 3415–3442 cm–1 is due to the OH stretching mode of hydroxyl groups, though the bands at 2917–2923 and 2360–2426 cm–1 are credited to CH interface with the surface of carbon and Si-H band because of the collaboration of acid with filler silica. The peak at 1587–1639 cm–1 is ascribed to the stretching of C = C. The piercing peak at 1382.73 cm–1 is attributable to symmetric and asymmetric winding modes of CH vibrations. The peak at 1269–1271 cm–1 is accredited to CO asymmetric stretching, whereas many feeble peaks within 450–750 cm–1 represent out-of-plane CH bending modes [184]. Also, The FTIR spectrum of AC produced from waste tires by H2O2 activation revealed peaks around 3400, 1700, 1627, and 1580 cm−1 attributable to the OH cluster of phenols and CO bonds. A band at 3400 cm−1 was related to hydroxyl groups of OH vibrations, while the peak for distinctive widening vibrations of carboxyl groups occurred at 1700 cm−1, validating the existence of CO. The peaks at 1627 and 1580 cm−1 are characteristic of thin stretching vibrations of aromatics. The peak at 1180 cm−1 indicates the company of the carbonyl to an ester [186].

The FTIR spectra of carbon dots demonstrated a robust, broad peak at 3361 cm−1 related to O − H stretching vibration, signifying the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl clusters ensuing in the hydrophilic actions of the samples. The peak at 2100 cm−1 is linked to C = C bending vibration from the aromatic assembly, while the peak at 1377 cm−1 is credited to −COOH stretching vibration. The occurrence of nitro (NO2) groups was established by two peaks at 1639 cm−1 and 600 cm−1 allocated correspondingly to nitro assemblies' symmetric and asymmetric stretching. Also, the outcomes propose the attendance of carboxyl hydroxyl (OH), (COOH), and nitro (NO2) functional sets on the carbon surface [79].

The FTIR spectrum was used to characterize polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes [189]. The asymmetric CH stretching vibrations of CH2 groups in pure polyethylene oxide exhibited a strong peak at 2874.90 cm−1 in its FTIR spectrum. The spectrum also showed a significant central peak at 1468.33 cm−1 due to asymmetric bending vibrations of CH2 groups and a strong and intense peak at 1102.06 cm−1 due to the asymmetric C–O–C stretching for epoxide groups. These configurations are consistent with the structural features of polyethylene oxide polymer. The FTIR spectrum of CuO nanoparticles has a major peak at 607.90 cm−1. The spectrum of multi-walled carbon nanotubes has two strong bands at 3435 and 1717 cm−1, representing the O–H and C = O of carboxylic acids, respectively, which confirms the successful oxidation of multi-walled carbon nanotubes. The spectra of polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes: 1% and polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes: 3% nanocomposites have peaks corresponding to CuO, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, and polyethylene oxide, verifying the presence of these components in the composite samples. However, the intensity of the peaks at 2874.90 and 1102.06 cm−1 is slightly reduced in the case of polyethylene oxide–CuO–multi-walled carbon nanotubes: 3% nanocomposite, which may be due to the interaction between the ether group of the polyethylene oxide and the hydroxyl one of the oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes, which are linked through hydrogen bonding. The interaction is stronger in the composite with 3% multi-walled carbon nanotubes, resulting in a more noticeable decrease in peak intensity compared to the composite with 1% multi-walled carbon nanotubes. As mentioned above, all the configurations in the nanocomposite samples have peaks at respective wavelengths.

Thermal solidity and likely reactions at advanced temperature feat were assessed by thermogravimetric and differential thermal analysis curves attained at N2 atmosphere for AC from terephthalaldehyde and o-phenylenediamine [139]. The early weight loss of 29.0% happens within 100 and 200 °C, along an endothermic outcome in the DTA curve. This section is credited to imine-linkage development through the condensation of aldehydic and amine sets accompanied by the loss of adsorbed moisture and hydrogen-stuck water molecules. KOH contributes significantly to accelerating dehydration and condensation reactions at low temperatures. At 220 °C, a stable section is detected till 400 °C, indicating the effective development of heat-firm polymer chains. But, at a temperature rise above 400 °C, a weight loss of 14.6% results from the pyrolysis of the ensuing polymer. A sequence of chemical reactions occurs at this temperature region, creating intramolecular construction and polymer chain breaching. This results in merged loop carbon assemblies with nitrogen atoms at ends, bends, and defects. The dropped weight loss at advanced temperature signifies the thermal rigidity and stability of these p-conjugated polycyclic merged rings.

The role of KOH as a catalyst enhances intermolecular condensation reactions in biomass, retarding the thermal ruin and resulting in high yield. Hence, KOH activation acts collaboratively as a porogen and thermal decay hindrance to advance carbon yield. The TGA dimension of pineapple crown powder, pre-carbonized at 250 °C, expressed three significant sample mass variation levels. The initial stage was a mass reduction of 11.99% from normal temperature to 261.4 °C [117]. The development that ensues is the breakdown of water content attended by the decay of composite compounds, for instance, cellulose. The additional step arises at temperatures between 261.4 and 318.7 °C and a loss in mass of 20.3%. This incidence is the breakdown of lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose. The decay of hemicellulose mass attaining 55% materializes at 200 °C and 300 °C, and loss of cellulose mass ensues from 260 to 390 °C. The course of lignin is relatively slow and begins with a temperature from 160 to 900 °C. The last step transpires at temperatures extending from 318.7 to 489 °C per mass loss of 21.64%. The residual sample is a carbon substance from the pineapple crown.

The effect of heating rate on waste tire AC was determined by thermogravimetric and differential thermogravimetric analysis [164]. The crushed rubber sample decayed the same way at the three diverse heating rates; the thermal degradation commenced at about 200 °C, with a reduced mass progressively within 300 and 500 °C. The ground rubber sample pyrolysis at the diverse heating rates was completed mainly at 500 °C, after which no additional weight loss was recorded. The pyrolysis deposit improved slightly with the rising heating rate, with a mean of 33 wt.% of the original weight. The lowest heating rate of 5 °C min −
1 delivered advanced residence time and led to a protracted decay compared to advanced heating rates. DTG analysis indicated the ground rubber sample to reduce weight in a wide temperature range from 200 to 550 °C. Other studies present lower temperatures within 200 and 300 °C to characterize the putrefaction of plasticizer, oils, and extracts constituted in the tire rubber and the second and third respective temperature ranges of 300 to 400 °C and 400 to 500 °C sections of weight loss signifying the decay of polybutadiene-styrene rubber, polybutadiene rubber, and natural rubber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental applications of ACs and OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their adsorption prowess, ACs and OCDs have been used widely in environmental applications for removing water, soil, and air contaminants. ACs are generally applied as powdered or granular AC. The powdered AC has a particle size of <0.18 mm with a mean length of 0.25 to 1.15 mm, while granular AC has asymmetrical particle sizes between 0.2 and 5 mm [47]. The different tendencies of adsorption efficiencies of diverse ACs and OCDs are attributable to their surface and chemical assets, such as their porosity, surface area, and chemistry (functional groups) that play critical roles in physisorption and chemisorption progressions [164] shown in Fig. 7 . Functional groups primarily are linked to the available heteroatoms on the ACs and OCDs surfaces categorized as oxygen-containing functional groups, nitrogen-containing functional groups, or sulfur-containing functional groups depending on the forms of existing heteroatoms [85,232].

The ability of ACs and OCDs to adsorb pollutants, for example, heavy metals, is due to their cation exchange dimensions, surface area, and surface-active functional groups [233]. Eliminating pollutants by ACs and OCDs involves complexation, electrostatic interaction, physical adsorption, precipitation, reduction, and ion exchange. Heavy metal removal by physical adsorption is triggered by the van der Waals forces occurring among heavy metal ions and surface molecules of ACs and OCDs [2,234]. Ion exchange is a non-precise adsorption mechanism owing to a low removal dimension that includes the physical exchange of ions and Coulombic force among heavy metal cations in an aqueous solution and negatively charged AC surface groups and OCDs [235]. Another mechanism of adsorption contingent on charges of electrostatic pull is electrostatic interactions among heavy metal surface charges and AC [236]. Other examples are found in Fig. 8 .

According to Kuroki et al. [238], there are three types of metal ions hard metal ions are called class A, soft metal ions are class B and their two borders are called a borderline class. The ions of class A are attached to hard bases, whereas the ions of class B are connected to soft bases. Three observed divalent metal ions were sorted into class B, Pb2+, and borderline class, Ni2+ and Zn2+. Based on the hard and soft acid and bases theory organization of hard/soft locations in graphene, oxygen functional assemblies at the edge of a graphene surface, for instance –COOH and –OH, are hard sites. The soft sites are located at the graphene's basal planes, which comprise π-orbitals on the surface polyaromatic components [238]. Likewise, in ACs, the hard sites are oxygen functional clusters, while the soft sites are the basal planes of graphite elements.

Consequently, class A metal ions tend to adsorb hard bases, and class B ions lean towards soft-based adsorption. Meanwhile, borderline class ions act as hard or soft ions, contingent on the pH of the solution. Therefore, Pb2+ inclines to adsorb on the soft bases of the π-electron cloud in the basal planes of the graphite surface in AC. Ni2+ and Zn2+ ions act as class A since the solution pH was at least four and are adsorbed on functional oxygen clusters, categorized as hard sites. The abundance of functional clusters in AC is widely less related to adsorption sites on the basal planes. Hence, the quantity of Ni2+ and Zn2+ at saturation is considerably reduced compared to Pb2+ [238].

The collaboration among AC functional assemblies and the species of chromium, i.e., trivalent and hexavalent chromium, in the adsorption course was studied by Valentín-Reyes et al. [239] in four mechanisms, as demonstrated in Fig. 9 . The first mechanism is associated to adsorption by elementary functional assemblies of anionic hexavalent chromium. The second mechanism indicates the reduction from Cr (VI) to Cr (III) by relating to functional clusters that function as electron donors. The third mechanism creates a three-step surface interface: (a) Cr (VI) is adsorbed on elementary positions by anion adsorption, though (b) through an adjacent electron donor it might be reduced to Cr (III), and then (c) via electrostatic repulsion Cr (III) is delivered to the solution. The fourth mechanism includes a cationic exchange of Cr (III) adsorption on acid locations in a joined reduction-adsorption mechanism.

AC produced from sugarcane bagasse by physical activation with N2 was improved by HNO3 oxidation and explored for Pb elimination [240]. The results showed that the adjustment by HNO3 enhanced the oxygen-comprising functional clusters (i.e., carboxyl, hydroxyl, ester, and carbonyl) on the surface of the adsorbent for the elimination of heavy metal ions (Pb2+) in wastewater. The extreme removal ability for Pb2+ was about 212.31 mg/g, representing 84.15%, and desorption efficiency of 94.85%. Zerovalent Fe nanoparticles prepared from tea liquor effectively removed 99% of As3+ from wastewater [81]. The adsorption efficiency of AC made from Camellia oleifera cake was examined for nickel ions adsorption from wastewater [241]. The activation was carried out using Na2CO3 and NaOH. The maximum adsorption by the AC with Na2CO3 and NaOH activations of Ni2+, respectively, was 95.7% and 86.75%. Further comparison of the efficiency of the adsorption of metals is exemplified in Table 5 . It is observed that the chemical activation of neem leaves by H3PO4 activation produced a 100% removal rate for Pb2+, physical activation of rice husk resulted in 91.8% removal for Cr3+, and Fe nanoparticles from tea extracts presented 100% removal for Cr6+. The highest removal rate for metals by ranking was by both chemical activation and Fe nanoparticles. However, lower rates for other metals, Cu, Cd, Zn, Ni, and Cr, were recorded by H3PO4 activation, as compared to the others.

The effectiveness of various adsorbents in terms of the adsorption of metals from wastewater can be predicted using AI tools such as ANN. For instance, a study by Dil et al. [260] demonstrates the effectiveness of zinc (II) oxide nanorods loaded on AC (ZnONRs-AC) as a cheap and high-quality adsorbent that can remove heavy metal ions and dyes with high efficiency by using ultrasonic waves to enhance the adsorption process. A six-factor, five-level central composite design based on response surface methodology (RSM) was employed to optimize the different factors that affect the ultrasonic-assisted simultaneous removal of heavy metals and dyes. The optimal conditions for achieving more than 97% removal of heavy metals and dyes with ZnONRs-AC were determined to be 25, 24, 18, and 14 mg L − 1 of Cd2+, Co2+ ions, methylene blue and crystal violet dye concentrations, respectively, 0.025 g of ZnONRs-AC and 5.1 min of sonication time. The removal performance of ZnONPs-AC in the simultaneous removal of heavy metals and dyes was accurately predicted by using a three-layer neural network with 14 neurons in the hidden layer and a Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm for back propagation.

In another study, copper oxide nanoparticle-loaded AC (CuONP-AC) was prepared and used effectively as a powerful adsorbent for the ultrasound-enhanced simultaneous removal of the Pb2+ ion and malachite green dye [261]. It was found that the ultrasonic power and CuONPs-AC together form a fast, cheap, and accurate adsorption method for removing large amounts of Pb2+ ions and malachite green dye from water. The best ultrasound-enhanced simultaneous removal of Pb2+ ions and malachite green dye was obtained by RSM and ANN (R2 = 0.99970 and 0.99994, respectively, for ultrasound-enhanced simultaneous removal of Pb2+ ion and malachite green dye) by conducting the experiments with initial Pb2+ concentration of 25 mg L − 1, initial malachite green concentration of 20 mg L − 1, pH 6.0, adsorbent dosage of 0.02 g and ultrasound exposure time of 5 min. This work has the benefits of high pollutant removal, low adsorbent usage, and a quick adsorption process at neutral pH.

Also, Franco et al. [262] report a preliminary investigation on the adsorption of indium (III) by carbon-based materials, such as AC, hydroxylated multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT–OH), and carboxylated multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT–COOH). The main aim was to develop an adaptive ANFIS and an ANN to estimate the adsorption capacity of other materials under different operating conditions. Both models consider the type of adsorbent, the amount of adsorbent, and the contact time to predict the adsorption capacity, which ranged from 12.896 to 981.000 mg g − 1, using 72 records. Both modeling approaches can fit the experimental data, considering the statistical values obtained. The ANFIS achieved the best performance when the hybrid method was chosen, resulting in an R2 of 0.9998 and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 48,373 with 250 epochs. On the other hand, the ANN achieved the best performance when using the Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm, reaching an R2 of 0.9831, MSE of 0.0180, and nine epochs. Considering the modeling and experimental aspects, it shows that the increase in the amount of adsorbent reduced the adsorbent capacity. The increase in the contact time increased the adsorption capacity until it reached equilibrium. The comparison between the experimental and ANN-predicted data confirmed that ANN can also be used to predict the indium (III) adsorption capacity as a function of the input variables.

The adsorptive efficiency of AC from waste tires indicated efficient removal of atrazine and ibuprofen from wastewater [164]. The raw and the CO2-ACs displayed low adsorption measurements for both adsorbates, whereas the KOH and KOHCO2-ACs presented superior adsorption dimensions for the two adsorbates. Increasing the initial concentration of atrazine from 5 to 20 mg/L led to a slight increase in the adsorption capacity of the materials from chemical activation and combined chemical-physical activation, with respective maximum values recorded at 90% and 95%, respectively. However, using the raw or physically activated materials resulted in lesser atrazine elimination but improved correspondingly from 8 to 28% and 0–21% upon increasing the initial concentration of atrazine. The elimination competence of ibuprofen for the various adsorbents was markedly inferior to that of atrazine. Advanced elimination effectiveness was recorded for carbons by chemical activation and combined chemical-physical activation. Among the diverse established activation approaches, chemical activation formed char with a highly advanced surface area and micro/mesoporosity, resulting in better adsorptive efficiencies. In contrast, physical activation produced less improved textural assets of the raw char and, consequently, the adsorptive efficiency [164].

A novel Fe/Pd nanoparticles synthesized from starch successfully removed 98% of trichloroethylene from wastewater [81]. AC synthesized separately from solid olive waste using KOH, H3PO4, and steam as activating agents removed indigo carmine dye in an aqueous medium [263]. KOH-AC, H3PO4-AC, and steam-AC adsorbed 599, 505, and 495 mg/g of the dye, respectively. The adsorption of AC formed from different types of plants and date seeds using chemical and physical activation methods was evaluated [161]. Impregnation with KOH generated superior yields than H3PO4. The maximum adsorption was determined for nine diverse kinds of date seeds. The Khalas seed type resulted in the maximum methylene blue adsorption measurements of 165 mg of methylene blue per gram of activated date seeds. There was a significant difference between several kinds of seeds concerning adsorption presentation. The activated date seeds conveyed inferior adsorption abilities of 165.1 mg/g for methylene blue compared to 230 mg/g commercial AC. Also, lesser oil adsorption was detected for activated date seeds with 191 mg/g compared to commercial AC with 622 mg/g. The comparison of the efficiency of the adsorption of dyes is represented in Table 6 . The results reveal chemical activation of Pisum sativum pods by ZnCl2 and KOH activation to remove 100% of acid orange 7 dye, physical activation of coconut shells removed 98.64% of methylene blue, and Fe polyphenol nanoparticles from E. tereticornis, M. nesophila, and R. officinalis removed 100% of acid black dye. The optimum adsorption rate for dyes by ranking was recorded by chemical activation and Fe polyphenol nanoparticles.

AI techniques can be used to optimize the adsorption process of dyes and organic pollutants from water. For instance, Ghaedi et al. [70] synthesized copper sulfide nanoparticles loaded on AC (CuS-NP-AC) using a green method and tested the material's suitability for removing reactive orange 12 (RO-12). Principal component analysis-ANN (PCA-ANN) was used to maximize the impacts of factors. With a high adsorption capacity of 96.9 mg g − 1, the tiny amount of 0.017 g of the suggested adsorbent may successfully remove reactive orange (Removal efficiency > 95%) in a short time (31.09 min). Also, Salehi et al. [71] compared response surface methodology (RSM) and ANN for removing crystal violet dye with magnetic AC. They evaluated the performance of central composite design (CCD) and ANN models using R2 and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE). They reported a lower percent MAPE for ANN (0.38) than for CCD (0.59), suggesting that ANN was more capable and accurate in predicting the removal process and, in addition, using ANN and genetic algorithm, Karimi & Ghaedi [278] modeled and optimized the adsorption process of methylene blue on AC. They used ANN trained by genetic algorithm to find the best operating conditions for maximum methylene blue removal. Their results showed that this technique was effective for optimizing the adsorption parameters. Likewise, Gar Alalm & Nasr [72] used an ANN with a 4–5–1 structure to estimate the adsorption capacity of AC prepared from casuarina charcoal for chlorothalonil pesticide removal. They found that the ANN model had a high accuracy and that pH was the most critical factor affecting adsorption. They also calculated the cost of an adsorption unit with a capacity of 100 m3 d − 1 and a chlorothalonil concentration of 250 ± 50 mg L − 1, which was $1.18 m − 3, based on amortization and operating costs.

According to Ozkan et al. [73], salicylic acid may be effectively extracted from aqueous solutions utilizing AC. By adjusting the following parameters: mass of AC, contact duration t, pH, and initial concentration of salicylic acid in aqueous solution, the RSM and ANN models were created to estimate the extraction efficiency of salicylic acid. Using the RSM model, the significance of each parameter and its synergistic interacting effects on the elimination of salicylic acid were described with the least number of experiments. Both models accurately predicted the removal efficiency of salicylic acid and closely matched the removal efficiency observed in experiments. On the other hand, ANN outperformed RSM in terms of accuracy. However, there were very few marginal errors between the models. An experimental removal effectiveness of 91% was observed under the ideal conditions: concentration of salicylic acid = 17 mg/dm3, contact time = 70 min, mass of AC = 25 mg, and pH = 4, as predicted by the RSM model. Additionally, the models performed better when used with unseen data, demonstrating the validity of their generalization skills. Therefore, the study on modeling, experimentation, and optimization of the salicylic acid adsorption process may be very important for designing and assessing the effectiveness of similar systems.

Shahryari et al. [279] used a three-layer FFBPNs with the Levenberg–Marquardt learning algorithm to model the adsorption of phenol on AC. They conducted batch experiments to collect data and trained the neural network with five input parameters: amount of adsorbent, initial phenol concentration, temperature, contact time, and pH. The output parameter was the final concentration of phenol. They improved the ANN performance by selecting the best transfer function and hidden neuron number. They also derived an empirical equation from the optimized network weights, which can be used to estimate the adsorption results without running the ANN software. Statistical measures, including mean absolute error (MAE), MSE, RMSE, and R2, were also used to assess the accuracy of the created ANN model. The ANN model was highly accurate in predicting the experimental data, with MSE, MAE, RMSE, and R2 of 0.1540, 0.0565, 0.2378, and 0.9998, respectively. The ANN model outperformed the conventional models in modeling the phenol adsorption on AC under various operating conditions.

The removal efficiencies of environmental availability of chlordecone from the soil by ACs from oak wood (Quercus ilex) and coconut shell (Cocos nucifera) were determined [280]. The precursors were activated chemically and physically with H3PO4, CO2, and steam. Soil modification with all the prepared ACs caused a reduction in the transfer of chlordecone by a minimum of 65% except for coconut shell steam AC, which presented a 47% elimination. These reveal the significance of the difference in precursor matrix, degree of porosity, and pore assembly in the soil's retaining course of chlordecone.

AC derived from Arachis hypogaea (groundnut) removed benzimidazole fungicide carbendazim from seven diverse soils [281]. The activation was done chemically using H2SO4. The maximum elimination was 70% in 5 ppm initial carbendazim concentration. The effective removal of phenanthrene and retrieval of Tween 80 surfactant from soil washing waste was explored by H3PO4 AC produced from waste walnut shells. The data demonstrated that the maximum selective removal of phenanthrene occurred at neutral pH with an AC dose of 0.3 g/L and a concentration of Tween 80 of 5 g/L. The maximum selective adsorption of phenanthrene and Tween 80 recovery were 95% and 90%, respectively. The connections among oxygen comprising functional groups of the AC and phenanthrene and π-π relations were mutually vital in the adsorption course. The prepared AC can be restored using ethanol and reused twice with a phenanthrene removal capacity of 80% [45]. The efficiency of granular AC from apricot shells, combined with diatomite, for the remediation of petroleum-contaminated soils was explored [46]. The results showed that ordinary attenuation reduced the amount of petroleum hydrocarbons in the soils in two months by 50% and in 13 months by 72%. Effectual adsorption of petroleum hydrocarbons in soil was realized using the combination of AC and diatomite with reduction capacities of 90% in two months and 92% in 13 months and low soil phytotoxicity throughout the incubation.

Additionally, the amendment generates improved settings for plant growth and degrading microorganisms owing to adjustable adsorption of toxic petroleum fragments and metabolites, a surge in soil water-holding dimensions, permeability, and amplified resistance by plants to abiotic strain. The elimination efficiency of chromium in polluted soil by iron-loaded AC from coconut shells was examined [282]. The results revealed the elimination effectiveness hinges on the adsorbent dose ratio and the period for remediation. The maximum Cr (VI) elimination competence was 80.2% at a dose ratio of 5% and a remediation period of 10 days. The adsorption mechanisms for Cr (VI) elimination included electromigration of Cr (VI) and Cr (III), adsorption/electrosorption of Cr (VI) on the constituent parts of the AC complex, and reduction of Cr (VI) to Cr (III). Juglans regia-produced AC adsorption dimensions were examined for extracting fungicide ethaboxam from soils [283]. The walnut shell-AC was produced using H2SO4 as an activation agent. The maximum remediation of ethaboxam was attained at five ppm of 99%. This method is economical, environmentally benign, and simply applicable. The removal efficiency of triasulfuron from the soil by AC produced from sugarcane bagasse was analyzed [284]. The elimination was done using two pesticide concentrations from the soil samples. The highest adsorption of 44% of triasulfuron was detected at 5 ppm concentration, while 63% adsorption was recorded at 7.5 ppm concentration. This method of soil remediation of triasulfuron was advantageous both environmentally and economically.

The soil's Cr(VI) content was reduced by 66% by applying biochar and nanoscale zero-valent iron [285]. It was found that 1 g of nanoscale zero-valent iron injection into the contaminated soil reduced 28% of 1 kg Cr(VI) mass. Moreover, 98% of Cr(VI) was eliminated in 24 h at pH 5. The carboxymethyl cellulose stabilizer and nanoscale zero-valent iron combination also showed high capacity in transforming most of the Cr(VI) contaminants into carbonate-bound and Iron-Manganese oxides-bound, which lowered the bioavailability and leachability of Cr by 50% at 1 g to 10 mL of soil to solution condition [26]. This combination also significantly improved in removing organic contaminants such as dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane (DDT), trichloroethylene, and pesticides from the soil column. For example, 44% of trichloroethylene was dechlorinated in 30 h by injecting stabilized nanoscale zero-valent iron into the potting soil with 9.2% organic matter in the soil. By injecting 20% aqueous nanoscale zero-valent iron in 1 kg of soil, 25% of DDT was removed in 72 h from 1 kgs of soil that had 24 mg of DDT per kg soil. A higher amount of nanoscale zero-valent iron was required to remediate the soils that had been contaminated for a long time.

The effective remediation of CH4 and CO2 was examined using three ACs produced with olive stones using H3PO4 chemical activation and the other by CO2 and physical steam activations [44]. The results revealed superior adsorption of CO2 compared to CH4 for all the ACs. This character is distinctive to adsorption by AC, resulting from the incidence of a quadrupole moment on CO2 molecule stimulating robust adsorptive/adsorbent interactions. Also, for the two adsorbents, H3PO4 chemical activation presented advanced adsorption dimensions in the order of H3PO4-AC> steam-AC > CO2-AC. The adsorption efficiencies of the three adsorbents, H3PO4-AC, steam-AC, and CO2- AC for CH4 were 6.518, 3.913, and 5.417 mol/kg, respectively, while that for CO2 was 10.873, 5.878, and 7.968 mol/kg. The H3PO4 – activation of the precursor grants a well-developed total pore volume, surface area, and micropore volume, leading to advanced adsorption measurements for both CH4 and CO2. During the adsorption of CH4, mesopores enabled the diffusion of the gas particles into the micropores. In contrast, in the adsorption of CO2, supplementary oxygen groups on the steam-AC boosted its adsorption efficiency.

AC from bamboo was fashioned over a single-step H3PO4 activation to eliminate CO2 [77]. The connection among acid-impregnated bamboo precursors at various concentrations from 50 to 70 wt.% was observed. The AC produced by 50 wt.% H3PO4 activation demonstrated the maximum adsorption dimensions of CO2 of 1.45 mmol/g at 25 °C/1 bar and 9.0 mmol/g at 25 °C/30 bar. AC formed from oak leaves by KOH chemical activation in consolidation through heat treatment from 600 to 900 °C for removal of CO2 was studied [201]. The adsorption capacity of the shaped AC for CO2 was 6.17 mmol/g and 5.44 mmol/g at 0 and 25 °C, respectively. The adsorbent also displayed a decent CO2/N2 selectivity and fast adsorption kinetics that can be restored with greater cyclic steadiness after several cycles. AC from the andiroba shell was investigated as an economical, renewable biomass supply for the adsorption of CO2 [151]. The activation was by CO2 physical activation at 1 h, 5 h, and 10 h at 880 ºC. The adsorbent produced at 5 h exhibited decent microporosity, corresponding to the maximum adsorption measurement of CO2 as 3.2 mmol/g and 6.1 mmol/g at 25 ºC and 0 ºC up to 1 bar. The produced adsorbent at 5 h also expressed a good selectivity for CO2/N2 and fast adsorption kinetics that can be readily renewed with better cyclic solidity after many cycles.

ACs from molasses products of crushed sugar cane for the adsorption of CO2 were formed through an activation with H2SO4, H3PO4, HCl, NaOH, and KOH [286]. The process is cost-saving. The optimum adsorption for CO2 of 5.18 mmol/g at 0 °C at 1 bar was attained using KOH-AC. The excellent adsorption of CO2 by KOH-AC was ascribed to its high microporosity. However, HCl-AC presented relatively high CO2 adsorption and low microporosity. The surface of the HCl-AC lacks acid species, encouraging the adsorption of CO2. The efficiency of adsorption for the adsorbents was KOH AC (5.18 mmol/g) > H2SO4 AC (4.44 mmol/g) > H3PO4 AC (2.82 mmol/g) > NaOH AC (1.92 mmol/g) > HCl AC (1.77 mmol/g).

Porous AC from bamboo shoots was formed by a two-step process of carbonization and K2CO3 activation to capture CO2 [287]. The results showed that the AC could efficiently adsorb CO2, corresponding to 6.03 and 3.44 mmol/g at 0 °C and 25 °C at 1 bar. The adsorbent, too, displayed decent cycle stability and selectivity to CO2 and N2. An earlier paper evaluated the result of chemical activation on adsorptive capture of CO2 using AC produced from eucalyptus camaldulensis wood [288]. The ACs were made with H3PO4 and ZnCl2 as activating agents and extra activation with KOH at various impregnation ratios and pyrolysis. The results for CO2 adsorption onto ZnCl2 AC revealed that the amount adsorbed increases with increasing BET surface area and mesoporosity.

AC discs with advanced surface area and ordered pore structures were produced from petroleum tar pitch and coal filler with KOH activation to remove N2, CH4, and CO2 [43]. The adsorbent demonstrated massive selectivity for CO2 (8.88 mol/kg) over CH4 (5.48 mol/kg) and N2 (3.08 mol/kg). Also, KOH activation prepares AC from petroleum coke to eliminate greenhouse gases such as CO2, CH4, and N2 [219]. The adsorption efficiency of the prepared carbon for CO2, CH4, and N2, respectively, was 4.14, 1.96, and 0.69 mmol/g. The results reveal that the adsorption course comprises the equilibrium removal mechanism reliant on the adsorbate molecular assets, for instance, dipole moment, polarizability, quadrupole moment, and kinetic diameter.

Multi-walled amine-functionalized carbon nanotubes were produced for the adsorption of CO2 from air [289]. The amine-functionalized carbon nanotubes accomplished maximum adsorption of CO2 at 96.3 mg/g. The removal efficiency of air pollutants from mobile sources through carbon nanotubes was examined [290]. The adsorption competencies of carbon nanotube filters extended from 10% to 95% for the loadings of 0.2–1.6 mg/cm2. CO, NOx, and CO2 removal competencies ranged from 5% to 60% for comparable carbon nanotube loadings. A multifunctional metal-organic framework and carbon nanotube-modified filter with joint ultrafine dust capture and SO2 adsorption capabilities was synthesized for indoor air purification [291]. The modified carbon nanotube presented a tremendously high capture efficiency of about 99.997% for ultrafine dust and SO2 adsorption dimensions in energetic filtration. Transition metal (Fe, Co, and Ni) nanocrystals encapsulated into nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes were formed and found useful for the adsorption of CO2 from air [292]. The adsorption capacities for the nanotubes respectively were 0.6–7.4 mmol/g (nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes), 1.5–15.4 mmol/g (Fe/Fe3-nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes), 1.1–14.0 mmol/g (Co-nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes); 1.0–13.2 mmol/g (Ni-nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes). Single-walled carbon nanotubes were successful in the removal of SO2 and N2 as 17.5–34 mmol/g (SO2) and 0.4–2.3 mmol/g (N2) in air [293]. Double-walled carbon nanotubes proved useful for the selective removal of CO2, H2S, SO2, and N2 2.54 mmol/g (CO2); 0.22 mmol/g (H2S); 5.5 mmol/g (SO2); 0.42 mmol/g (N2) in air [294].

A novel method to maximize the capture of gas pollutants by ACs and OCDs is the integration of AI approaches. For example, ANN was investigated by Kumar et al. [295] to simulate the hydrogen adsorption isotherms on AC with different heteroatoms. They built a feed-forward neural network with a linear output layer and a hidden layer with a hyperbolic tangent activation function to imitate the hydrogen adsorption isotherms on pitch-based carbons with various heteroatom configurations. With little input, the neural network demonstrated good accuracy in replicating the hydrogen experiment data on carbon materials. Regardless of the input conditions, the discrepancy between the anticipated hydrogen uptake capacity and the experimental data was less than 5% during the training phase. For the new inputs, the equilibrium hydrogen intake may be modeled by the ANN. The ANN demonstrated resilience and responsiveness to various input conditions throughout the training and testing. Also, in a trickling bed reactor, Ozkan et al. [73] estimated the SO2 removal efficiency of AC using an ANN and compared the ANN model to a statistical model. With comparatively little prediction error, the ANN could predict the attributes. For the data set used to construct the network, the neural network model's MSEs were less than those of the statistical regression model. The current work shows how the theoretical findings may be used in practice to develop and scale up safer trickle bed reactor processing and control. Similarly, Khoshraftar & Ghaemi [296] produced microporous AC from pistachio shells by physical activation for CO2 capture. They used RSM to optimize the CO2 adsorption capacity as a function of pressure and temperature. They also developed feed-forward multilayer perceptron (MLP) ANN models using back-propagation to model CO2 adsorption. The ANN and RSM models were effective in predicting CO2 adsorption. Likewise, ANN was applied to estimate the CO2 adsorption capacity of AC produced by KOH activation [297]. The ANN model effectively modeled CO2 adsorption on the AC, which is relevant for mitigating the greenhouse effect and climate change. The model results showed good agreement with the process performance. In addition, gas masks impregnated with ACs or OCDs are specialized respiratory personal protective equipment that filter out noxious gases, odors, and chemical vapors from the air in occupational settings or accidental or planned explosions. AC and OCD filters have other applications, such as in aircraft cabin air filtration and ventilation systems, to improve indoor air quality.

The mechanism of the adsorption courses can be presented via the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order [98,99] models. The kinetic models are represented in linear forms given by Eqs. (19) and (20).

where qe and qt are the respective amount of adsorbate adsorbed (mg/g) at equilibrium and at any time t, t is the time (min), and k1 (1/min) and k2 (g/mg/min) are the equilibrium rate constants correspondingly of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models [98,99].

The models are exemplified by Somyanonthanakun et al. [240] using AC produced from sugarcane bagasse by physical activation, demonstrating the kinetic model for the adsorption of Pb2+ followed by the pseudo-second-order. Similarly, the pseudo-second-order was best suited for the adsorption of Ni2+ on AC made from Camellia oleifera cake by chemical activation [241]. Also, the adsorption mechanism for indigo carmine dye fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model using AC produced from solid olive waste infused with olive mill wastewater by chemical and physical activation [263]. In addition, AC produced using chemical and physical activation methods matched the pseudo-second-order model, showing that the adsorption course happened to ensure a chemical method instead of being done only through intraparticle dispersion for AC formed from different types of date seeds [161]. Likewise, the pseudo-second-order kinetic model produced from cactus fruit peels for removing Basic Red 46 cationic dye was the best fit by AC [116].

The adsorption progressions of AC from waste tires by steam activation followed pseudo-second-order kinetics [184]. Also, the adsorption course of Mn on steam-AC from waste tires is well-suited with a pseudo-second-order kinetics model, indicating that the adsorption course comprises several diffusion steps [185]. Similarly, the adsorption kinetic data for Cu(II) adsorption on AC from petroleum coke by KOH activation fits well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model [210]. Likewise, the kinetic adsorption analysis for methylene blue by AC from scrap tires by chemical activation suited the pseudo-second-order model [212]. Also, the pseudo-second-order kinetic model was fitting for polyacrylonitrile-derived AC fibers for removing perfluorooctane sulfonate and perfluorooctanoate [228].

The adsorption kinetics model for several OCDs, including magnetized graphene oxide, magnetically AC, magnetic halloysite nanotube nano-hybrid, and nanocomposite of poly acrylic acid, well fitted to the pseudo-second-order [1,298–300]. However, other OCDs suited the pseudo-first-order, such as nanocomposite of poly acrylic acid and carboxymethyl-cellulose-Fe3O4/SiO2 [1,301].

Experimental adsorption data is elucidated using isotherm models such as the Langmuir, Freundlich, Sip, Temkin, Khan, Redlich-Peterson, Radke-Prausnitz, Frenkel-Halsey-Hill, Toth, and Dubinin-Radushkevich isotherms [27,97–99,164]. The adsorption isotherm of AC produced from plants is exemplified using Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms. The Langmuir isotherm is generally functional to monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous adsorbent surface (monolayer adsorption model), and it is given in linear form as follows:

The Freundlich isotherm is practical for adsorption surfaces with a nonuniform energy supply (multilayer adsorption model), and it is given in linear rm as follows
