where qe is the amount of adsorbate adsorbed at equilibrium, Ce (mg/L) is the equilibrium concentration, KF (mg/g) represents the adsorption capacity, and n stands for intensity of adsorption [97–99,164]. The model is demonstrated using AC produced from sugarcane bagasse by physical activation, having the extreme removal ability for Pb2+ to be consistent with the Langmuir model [240]. Similarly, in a single-step chemical activation, the adsorptive efficiency of AC produced from millet cob husk showed the adsorption equilibrium data for methylene blue to fit the Langmuir isotherm best [207]. In contrast, the adsorption isotherm for indigo carmine dye using AC made from solid olive waste, impregnated with olive mill wastewater by chemical and physical activation fitted well with the linear Freundlich isotherm, indicating multilayer adsorption [263]. Also, the Freundlich model best fits the equilibrium data for Basic Red 46 cationic dye adsorption by AC made from cactus fruit peels [116].

The adsorption isotherm of Mn on steam-AC from waste tires is well-matched with the Freundlich model, demonstrating multilayers and heterogeneous adsorption [185]. Also, The investigation concerning Cu(II) adsorption on AC from petroleum coke by KOH activation well-suited the Freundlich model [210]. However, Cr(VI) and Mn(II) adsorption on AC from waste tires by steam activation matched Langmuir adsorption isotherm [184]. Likewise, the adsorption isotherm for Cd(II) and Pb(II) adsorption on AC from waste tires by H2O2 activation fitted the Langmuir model [186]. Furthermore, the equilibrium adsorption behavior for the adsorption of methylene blue by AC from scrap tires by chemical activation fitted the Langmuir isotherm model [212].

Similarly, the methylene blue adsorption data on AC produced from waste tires using physical and chemical activations followed the Langmuir isotherm model [213]. Additionally, the methylene blue adsorption isotherm of AC manufactured from petroleum coke using chemical activation matched the Langmuir model, demonstrating the monolayer adsorption mechanism [23]. However, the Freundlich model was fitting for removing perfluorooctane sulfonate and perfluorooctanoate by polyacrylonitrile-derived AC fibers [228].

Batch adsorption isotherms for several OCDs well fitted the Langmuir (monolayer) model, including magnetized graphene oxide, magnetic halloysite nanotube nano-hybrid, nanocomposite of poly acrylic acid, and carboxymethyl-cellulose-Fe3O4/SiO2 [1,299–301]. At the same time, others matched the Freundlich model, such as magnetic AC [298].

ACs and OCDs have been established as porous, solid, carbonaceous adsorbents synthesized from carbonaceous materials (biomass and non-biomass) at high temperatures under inert conditions. The distinctive synthesis of ACs comprises conventional and advanced techniques. Conventional synthesis involves one-step or two-step carbonization and activation procedures. At the same time, the advanced methods employ novel processes to tune known ACs and OCDs' chemical, physical, and optical properties to the desired structure, dimension, and use. The advanced techniques include hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted irradiation, solvothermal methods, and one-pot synthesis. Carbonization removes non-carbon constituents such as hydrogen and oxygen from the precursors through pyrolytic decomposition, resulting in a skeletal carbon with masked pore assembly. The standard methods used for activation are physical, chemical, and combinatorics. The combinatorial chemico-physical synthesis is more common in literature than the physico-chemical synthesis.

The review established that H3PO4, increasingly of late, is preferred among the chemical activation agents for the large-scale production of ACs because it offers some environmental advantages such as ease of recovery, low energy cost, and high carbon yield. Additionally, AC prepared by chemical activation procedures results in more modified surface chemistry and needed pore structures compared to physical activation. The returns of chemical activation over physical activation comprise faster activation time, lesser activation temperature, advanced surface area, and larger pore volumes.

However, ACs' limitations include slothful adsorption kinetics, nonspecific adsorption, and low competence in handling microorganisms, exemplified by poor microbiocidal and microbiostatic properties. These limitations have spurred the search for alternatives, such as transforming carbon materials to nano-scale levels and other derivatives presented in this review. OCDs are emergent and rapidly evolving materials mainly synthesized using advanced technologies to generate adsorbents such as bionanocomposites, carbon dots, carbon nanofibers, and carbon nanotubes. These products can compensate for the shortcomings of ACs.

ACs and OCDs’ structure and surface chemistry have been deliberated using N2 adsorption-desorption, SEM, XRD, and FTIR spectroscopy for product elucidation, which revealed their physical properties, nature, chemical reactivity of the surface functional groups, surface area, pore volume, extra dense constituents, crystalline nature, and thermal solidity.

The review has revealed that the advanced microwave-assisted physical activation of biomass precursors presented ACs with the highest specific surface area. In contrast, conventional chemical activation gave biomass ACs the highest total pore volume. Conventional chemically generated non-biomass ACs demonstrated the highest specific surface area and total pore volume. A comparison of the results from biomass and non-biomass ACs showed conventional chemically activated non-biomass yielded the highest specific surface area. In contrast, its corresponding biomass ACs produced the highest total pore volume.

The different tendencies of adsorption efficiencies of diverse ACs and OCDs are identified in this review. These are attributed to their surface and chemical assets, such as their porosity, surface area, and chemistry (functional groups), which play critical roles in physisorption and chemisorption progressions. This review has revealed that eliminating pollutants by ACs and OCDs requires various mechanisms such as complexation, electrostatic interaction, physical adsorption, precipitation, reduction, and ion exchange. Heavy metals removal by physical adsorption is triggered by the van der Waals forces occurring among heavy metal ions and surface molecules of ACs and OCDs.

Applying ACs and OCDs from biomass and non-biomass is established for removing water, soil, and air pollutants. By ranking, chemical activation by H3PO4 activation and Fe nanoparticles offered the highest removal rate for metals Pb and Cr6+, respectively. Also, the optimum adsorption rate for dyes by ranking was recorded by the chemical activation by ZnCl2 and KOH activation, and Fe polyphenol nanoparticles, respectively, for acid orange 7 dye and acid black dye. The adsorbents demonstrated massive selectivity for several gasses in the air, such as CO, CO2, CH4, N2, H2S, and SO2. They also effectively remediated soil contaminants such as chlordecone, benzimidazole fungicide carbendazim, phenanthrene, Tween 80, petroleum, Cr, and triasulfuron. However, though carbon nanoparticles present a considerable potential for environmental application, their environmental and health effects are not well established and must be used sparingly. In addition, the use of AI techniques in the advancement of novel AC materials and devices for environmental remediation has been identified.

In addition to providing scientific evidence on their syntheses and applications in environmental remediation, the review has discovered knowledge to bridge research gaps and provided future research dimensions of these treasured chemical substances of organic origin. Identifying that the environment is dynamic but susceptible to human-caused instabilities, ACs, and OCDs must not be perceived as a panacea to evolving and complex environmental pollution problems. The hope is to emphasize the limitations of these adsorbents and encourage the scientific community to seek innovative solutions, especially in specific polluted environments. There is a need to explore new materials or technologies for better alternatives. At the same time, more specific suggestions for future research dimensions to stimulate readers' interest and guide future research work have been provided.

Conventional and advanced techniques in modifying biomass and non-biomass resources have excessive potential to develop an environmentally benign course to manufacture an extensive diversity of products. Future research dimensions to seal the critical knowledge gaps are deliberated below.

The diverse chemical compositions of precursors are believed to substantially influence the surface alignment, interaction with the activation agent, and yield. A comprehensive evaluation of ACs and OCDs fashioned from diverse precursors using a combinatorial synthesis of different activation agents and activations will be immensely significant in classifying standard features and advanced fitting procedures for manufacturing the adsorbents. This calls for innovative research using advanced techniques like AI to optimize the combinations of processes for producing ACs and OCDs.

The chemical activation of carbon precursors usually produces AC with a high specific surface area. Nevertheless, this procedure creates a huge volume of wastewater containing dissolved alkali metals or acids that are detrimental to the environment. Although physical activation offers an option to environmentally friendly means, it falls short since a high temperature and extended time for activation is required. Hence, more research must establish suitable environmentally friendly means of AC production with optimized courses. Though carbon nanoparticles present considerable potential for environmental application, their environmental and health effects are not well-established and require further investigation.

Although recent attention has been given to manufacturing ACs and OCDs from inexpensive and renewable resources such as agricultural wastes, the cost of commercial ACs still seems high, which confines their extensive use. This, therefore, calls for further exploration of cheaper sources of carbon precursors.

Novel devices incorporating pollution-control fabrics impregnated with ACs and OCDs can be explored for domestic and office use to trap ambient air pollutants. Also, research in geoengineering focusing on integrating these adsorbents into high tensile strength fabrics for use in smokestacks, locomotive, and vehicular exhausts as filters to mitigate greenhouse gas forcing of climate on a planetary scale can be explored.

Research that targets the infusion of geotextiles with ACs and OCDs to reclaim land and eliminate contaminants, enhance soil fertility through amendment, and control odors and erosion, especially in developing countries where uncontrolled human-induced land degradation and pollution are prevalent. This intervention, supported by rigorous law enforcement, can improve ecological restoration in such countries.

Large-scale production of AC- and OCD-impregnated devices should be advanced through innovative science and technology to align with the circular economy ideals in the face of global environmental change. These devices include water pitchers, faucet filters, countertop filters, under sink filters, whole house filters, potable water filters, bag filters, cartridge filters, and smoke active carbon filters for removing various impurities such as chlorine, volatile organic compounds, roasting smoke, bacteria, protozoa, and some chemicals and to improve environmental quality.

While ANN model prediction of AC synthesis is common, it is rare with OCDs, which warrants attention. Nonetheless, environmental remediation applications that significantly benefit from the transformative power of AI are typical of these two broad carbon categories. Various AI models, including single and hybrid, have been able to forecast the effectiveness of diverse adsorbents for removing pollutants such as dyes, organic compounds, metals, drugs, pharmaceuticals, polychlorinated biphenyls, and pesticides from the environment. However, AI tools also have some limitations that must be resolved to maximize their potential in real-world environmental applications. Choosing appropriate data, using AI tools appropriately, and conducting more pilot plant studies will assist in overcoming these obstacles.

One of the main challenges of AI tools like ANNs is the low reproducibility caused by random weight and bias that may lead to a suboptimal solution. Different AI tools must be used to estimate the competence of adsorbents in removing pollutants through the adsorption course.

Isaac K. Tetteh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ibrahim Issahaku: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Antonia Y. Tetteh: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.
